1:1.text: Helcias, the high priest who discovered the book of the law, (Clement of Alexandria, Strom. i., etc.) though this be uncertain.
Anathoth, a village to the north of Jerusalem, to which many priests had retired, though it did not belong to them.

1:2.text: Year of the World 3375, Year before Christ 629.

1:3.text: Joakim. His immediate predecessor and successor, both called Jechonias, (Haydock) are passed over, as their reign was short, (Calmet) only lasting three months each.

Fifth. Jerusalem was taken on the 9th of the preceding month. But the temple was not burnt, nor the captives sent off till the fifth month, or the 10th of the civil year, Jeremias 39:8., and 52:13., and 4 Kings 25:2.
Jeremias also prophesied in Egypt, Jeremias 44.
But this title alludes to his principal predictions.

1:5.text: Knew, with affection, and designed thee for this office from eternity. Many think (Calmet) that Jeremias was purified from original sin before his birth.
He had this privilege, and was also a priest, prophet, virgin, and martyr.
Yet to sanctify, often means only to set aside, Exodus 13:2., and Ecclesiasticus 49:9.
Nations, whose overthrow he points out, Jeremias 25:27, 44, etc.

1:6.text: Ah. Hebrew ahah. Septuagint, "thou Being." Protestants, "Then said I: Ah, Lord God."
He does not imitate a child. He might be above 30 years old, though some say (Calmet) only 14, (Tirinus) or less; yet he finds himself devoid of eloquence, like Moses, Exodus 14:10.

1:9.text: Mouth; perhaps (Haydock) with a coal, by means of an angel, (Isaias 6:5.) in a sort of dream. He found himself changed into a new man.

1:10.text: Root up, to announce the fall and restoration of many nations, Ezechiel 4:2.
Jeremias spoke of the Gentiles, as well as of the Jews.

1:11.text: The almond-tree flourishes in January, and bears fruit in March.
Thus God will speedily send his scourge from Babylon, to punish his people. (Calmet) ---The sense is the same.
God's law is outwardly bitter, but the kernel is sweet.

1:13.text: North, whence the wind blows, to make the fire burn more intensely. Assyria lay to the north, and Babylon to the east of Judea; but the troops always penetrated the northern frontier, as they could not pass through the Desert Arabia. The caldron represented Jerusalem, Ezechiel 11:3., and 24:3.

1:14.text: North. The tributary kings were forced to attend, Judith 2:7., and 3 Kings 20:1.
About. The princes thus took Sedecias, and sent him to Reblatha, Jeremias 39:3.

1:15.text: North. The tributary kings were forced to attend, Judith 2:7., and 3 Kings 20:1.
About. The princes thus took Sedecias, and sent him to Reblatha, Jeremias 39:3.

1:17.text: Loins. Make haste, and take courage, Job 38:3.

1:19.text: Prevail. God does not promise peace, but victory.

2:2.text: Yet the Israelites at first shewed greater proofs of love than they did afterwards. It is true they often prevaricated, in the wilderness, Exodus 32:1., and Amos 5:25., and Psalm lxxvii., etc.
The Lord declares his gratuitous love, and then upbraids his people.
He had caused them to multiply in Egypt, and gratuitously made choice of them.

2:3.text: Increase; most desirable, Osee 9:10. God punished those who attempted to injure his people: yet they abandoned his service.

2:5.text: Vanity; idols, whom he will not mention, to spare their shame.

2:7.text: Carmel. That is, a fruitful, plentiful land.

2:8.text: Priests were silent, or abandoned themselves.
The land was full of false prophets, and none stood up for the Lord.

2:9.text: Children; the Israelites, under Moses, and their posterity transgressed.

2:10.text: Cethim; Macedon. It here denotes the western nations, as Cedar does those on the east.

2:11.text: Glory; the true God and his ark, Psalm 3:4., and 1 Kings 4:21.

2:13.text: Water. The idols and nations, to which they have had recourse, injure them.
The Jews did just the reverse to what God commanded.

2:14.text: Slave. From such, labour and tribute were required, Matthew 17:24., and John 8:33.

2:15.text: Lions; kings of Assyria, etc. The decree for the devastation of Juda had already passed, though it was not put in execution.

2:16.text: Taphnes, 16 miles from Pelusium, in Egypt. The nation proved only detrimental to the Jews, by engaging them in their abominations, instead of affording relief.

2:18.text: Troubled. Hebrew shichor, or Nile water, (Josue 13:3.) which was thought as good as wine, and grew better for keeping.
God often reproached the Jews for distrusting in his protection, and seeking aid from the Egyptians, who deceived them. We know not that Josias did so.
He even opposed them, and lost his life in defending the country, 4 Kings 23:29.
He was probably obliged to pay tribute to the Assyrians, whom Achaz had called in, 2 Paralipomenon 28:23. These alliances the Lord condemned.

2:20.text: Thou. Septuagint. Yet Hebrew and Chaldean have, "I have permissively broken," or foretold this infidelity.

2:21.text: Chosen. Hebrew, Sorek, Judges 16:4., and Isaias 5:2., and 16:8.
God created all things good, planted his Church in justice, and no evil proceeds from Him.

2:22.text: Borith. An herb used to clean clothes, and take out spots and dirt, (Challoner) like kali, soda, (Calmet) or soap.

2:23.text: Valley of Hinnom, under the very walls of Jerusalem.
Runner. Hebrew, "dromedary," which takes its name from its swiftness.
The female camel continues all day with the male, and cannot be approached.
Juda is represented as no less libidinous.

2:24.text: Away. The female may easily be found by the poisonous hippo-manes.
Thus Jerusalem is an impudent prostitute.

2:25.text: Foot, and the parts which modesty covers. My exhortations are slighted.
Despair opens the door to every sort of impurity, Ephesians 4:19.

2:26.text: Taken. Juda affected still to be very pious, ver. 23.

2:28.text: Cities. All were abandoned, Ezechiel 16:24., and Osee 10:1.

2:30.text: Prophets; Zacharias, (2 Paralipomenon 24:21.) Isaias, etc., Matthew 23:34.
Punishment is designed by God to cause people to repent.

2:31.text: See, or hear. The sword seems to be animated.
Lateward. Hebrew, "darksome land." Have I not heaped blessings on my people?

2:33.text: Protestants, "hast thou also taught the wicked ones thy ways."
Thou hast opened a school of vice.

2:34.text: Innocent children, immolated to Moloc, or people murdered, whose blood thou hast not concealed, 4 Kings 21:16.

2:37.text: Head, like the violated Thamar, 2 Kings 13:19. The king of Egypt was routed, when coming to assist Sedecias, Jeremias 37:3, 10.

3:1.text: It. Hebrew, "a saying." Septuagint, "If," etc., Deuteronomy 24:1.
Lovers. Septuagint, "shepherds," (Calmet) seeking aid from idols and foreigners.

3:2.text: On the hills, idolatry and immorality prevailed.
Ways, where harlots used to sit, Genesis 38:14., and Proverbs 7:9., and 33:27.
No sin is so great as to be irremissible to the true penitent.

3:3.text: Rain; about November, Deuteronomy 11:14. This drought happened under Josias, (chap. 5:24.) and was different from that specified, Jeremias 8:13., and 14:1.

3:5.text: Wilt. Hebrew, "will he," etc. The faithless people will not use this language, but do all the evil possible.

3:6.text: Days, before the 18th year. God now pronounces sentence (Calmet) on all.

Rebellious, a faithless wife. The kingdom of Israel gave way to idolatry first. But the people were less favoured than Juda, which had many prophets and good kings, the temple, etc. Moreover, the misfortunes of Israel did not open the eyes of their brethren.
Both the ten tribes, and the two tribes (Worthington) which adhered to the line of David, prevaricated.

3:10.text: Falsehood. Their reform was only apparent, under Joas, etc.

3:11.text: Soul, and appeared comparatively innocent, ver. 6., and Ezechiel 16:51.
Yet neither of them was excusable.

3:12.text: North. Samaria, or rather Assyria, where the people were captives.

3:13.text: Ways, dividing thy love, and adoring idols with me.

3:14.text: Kindred. Only few Israelites returned under Cyrus. The rest came by degrees, though it was more fully accomplished by the conversion of the Gentiles.

3:15.text: Pastors. Zorobabel, etc., but more particularly Christ and his apostles.

3:16.text: Ark. It was no longer at Jerusalem. The law and its figures cease.

3:17.text: All. Many might embrace the Jewish religion. But the predictions of the prophets on this head, were not verified, till the gospel was preached.
Then many embraced the faith: several captives also returned to God.

3:18.text: North. Septuagint add, "and from all places" where the Jews were in captivity.
Many thus returned to their own country, ver. 14.
Israel had been led into captivity sooner, but all were released at once.

3:19.text: Gentiles, which they all covet, or to which they are invited in the Church.
Me. On these conditions thou shalt be restored.

3:21.text: God. Hence proceed these miseries and lamentations.

3:23.text: Liars, unfruitful; and the idols have left us empty.
They were placed on the hills.

3:24.text: Confusion. Thus Baal was styled Boseth, contemptuously, Jeremias 11:13.

3:25.text: Shame. We are justly punished with exile, (Calmet) and death.

4:1.text: Blocks; idols, (Calmet) and other occasions of sin.

4:2.text: Thus thou wilt learn to confess one God.
If all were upright, oaths would be unnecessary, Matthew 5:34. But as they are not so, (Calmet) this may be a religious act.
Justice. These three conditions are essential.
An oath must attest God, and be used when a thing is true and of moment.

4:3.text: Thorns. Your misconduct hinders your advancement.

4:4.text: Hearts. Understand, and act with purity.

4:7.text: Robber. Nabuchodonosor attacks unjustly, (Calmet) though he be God's scourge.

4:10.text: Alas! Protestants, "Then I said: Ah! Lord God, surely thou hast deceived," etc., (Haydock) by permitting the people to follow false prophets, Isaias 61:17., and Psalm 43:19.

4:11.text: Desert of Arabia. It may designate Nechao, (2 Paralipomenon 35:20.) who slew Josias, after whose death all fell to ruin.

4:15.text: Ephraim. At Bethel and Dan the calves were adored.
But they were now no more.
The irruption of Nabuchodonosor is announced. He set himself up for a god.

4:16.text: Guards. The Chaldeans will besiege the city, and prevent provisions from entering, 4 Kings 25:1.

4:19.text: My. God, (St. Jerome) the people, (Grotius) or rather Jeremias speaks.

4:20.text: Moment. The cities are destroyed as easily as a tent.

4:22.text: Me, practically. They have not honoured me as they ought.
If they were indeed devoid of sense, they would be excused.

4:23.text: Void. Hebrew, "Thohu and bohu," like chaos, Genesis 1:2.

4:25.text: Gone. Beasts feel the wrath of God, and in a land uninhabited; not even birds will remain, Osee 4:3.

4:26.text: Carmel: the beautiful country of Palestine, (Haydock) Jerusalem, (Theodoret) or the mountain so called.

4:27.text: Destroy, when Jechonias was led captive. God allowed the people still eleven years to repent; and he afterwards restored the Jews.
He will never suffer his church to perish.

4:28.text: Repented. Only a finite being can do this. God's resolution is here unconditional and fixed.

4:30.text: Stone, or antimony, like Jezabel, (4 Kings 9:30.) to darken and enlarge the eye-lids.
Lovers. Infidel nations of Egypt, etc.

4:31.text: Child. Hebrew and Septuagint, "a first-born," when the pains are most acute.

5:1.text: Go, Jeremias, and you who publicly adhere to me.

Man. Sodom would have been spared for the sake of ten just people, Genesis 18:32. Before the reform of Josias, Jerusalem was strangely corrupted, though these expressions be hyperbolical, and spoken as it were in the heat of debate. Josias, Holda, and others, were living at this time, and renowned for their piety.

5:2.text: Falsely. Some may confess my name. But they swear falsely, Jeremias 4:2.
Unless the requisite conditions be observed, an oath is unlawful.

5:3.text: Return. The miseries with which, Achaz, etc., were afflicted, produced no amendment.

5:4.text: Foolish idiots, (Calmet) who have had no means of receiving instruction.
Such might be more excusable; but when the chiefs offend, the evil is irremediable.

5:6.text: The Chaldeans, Persians and Greeks afflict them.
The first beast may designate Nabuc: the second Nabuzardon: the third, Alexander [the Great?] or Epiphanes.

5:7.text: How can. I have only the character of judge left.

5:8.text: Stallions. Hebrew mashcim, (Haydock) "stretching out," as others translate, Ezechiel 23:20.
The horse is the most intemperate of all animals but man.

5:10.text: Destroy, at the first taking of the city, ver. 18., and Jeremias 4:27.
Branches. Children of Jechonias, (chap. 22:30.) and the people.

5:13.text: Them. The evil shall fall on the head of these impostors. So the wicked deride the prophets (Calmet) impugning the known truth.

5:14.text: Fire. Thou shalt declare their impending ruin.

5:15.text: Ancient. Nemrod founded the empire of Ninive and of Babylon, Genesis 10:10. Nabopolassar had succeeded to the ancient Assyrian and Chaldean sovereigns. Under his son, Nabuchodonosor, the dominions were much enlarged.

5:17.text: Devour. Hebrew, "destroy." They did not eat human flesh.

5:19.text: Own. You shall thus know the difference between the masters.

5:22.text: It. Nature and chance are improper terms. God regulates all, and has established those laws which preserve the world.

5:24.text: Rain, in autumn and in spring, Deuteronomy 11:14.
That of barley began at the Passover, and that of wheat ended before the feast of weeks, (Exodus 34:22., and Leviticus 23:10.; Calmet) or Pentecost.

5:26.text: Man. As some tyrants have done, though this implies (Calmet) the eagerness with which the wicked strive to corrupt mankind.

5:28.text: Of the widow, is not in Hebrew.
Fatherless. Protestants add, "yet they prosper, and they," etc.

5:29.text: Nation? It is contrary to God's justice not to punish the guilty.

5:31.text: Clapped. Hebrew, "came down to, or received in, or domineered by their hands." The priests concurred with these impostors for their own ends.

6:1.text: Strengthen. Hebrew also, "Flee, Benjamites, from the midst," etc. Jerusalem belonged partly to this tribe, which prided itself on its valour.
Thecua was in the lot of Juda, 15 miles south of Jerusalem.

6:3.text: Shepherds. So captains are styled, because they feed and govern soldiers.

6:4.text: Prepare. Literally, "sanctify." Observe the usual formalities of demanding reparation, offering victims, etc. The Chaldeans thus encourage one another, and will allow themselves no rest day or night, ver. 5.

6:7.text: Cold. Hebrew, "come forth." All the citizens imitate her vices.

6:8.text: Instructed, by afflictions and prosperity, by public and internal admonitions. Willful ignorance is criminal, and drives God away, Jeremias 51:9., and Psalm 80:12.

6:9.text: Basket. Chaldeans, destroy all. Nabuchodonosor took Joakim twice, and afterwards Jechonias and Sedecias, Jeremias 29:2.

6:10.text: Non vocat impossibilitas supplicio quae de contemptu et infidelitate descendit.

6:11.text: In. Jeremias is indignant. The Lord then orders him to pour, or declare what will ensue.

6:14.text: Disgracefully. They shall be ashamed, when the contrary to what they declared shall come to pass, Jeremias 8:11.

6:16.text: Paths, of patriarchs, and of Moses. Avoid novelty in religion.
This advice is very applicable to the present generation.

6:18.text: Congregation. Septuagint, "shepherds feeding their flocks have heard." The Chaldeans are apprised of my resolution against Juda.
After the Jews had been called repeatedly in vain, the Gentiles obey, Matthew 10:5.

6:20.text: To me. For want of proper dispositions; otherwise the offerings of the best things were enjoined, and commendable. The Jews are taught not to depend on external observances, while they neglect the heart.

6:22.text: North. As Babylon lay with respect to Jerusalem, (Worthington) or rather to the east.

6:26.text: Only son. Let thy grief be extraordinary, Amos 8:10., and Zacharias 12:10.

6:27.text: Trier of my people, as of gold in the furnace, Job 22:25.

6:29.text: Lead, which was intermixed to purify the dross (St. Jerome) of gold and silver. But brass and iron could not be thus purified. All would be lost labour. Afflictions do not correct the obstinate.

7:2.text: Gate, before all the people. This discourse ends [in] Jeremias 13.

7:4.text: Lord. The triple repetition shews the vain confidence of the people, who blindly imagined that the temple would screen them, (Calmet) and that external sacrifices would suffice. But they were rejected with the temple.

7:7.text: Dwell. Hebrew and Septuagint as [in] ver. 3.

7:9.text: Not, which have done you no good, and deserve not to be styled gods.

7:10.text: Because. Hebrew also, "although," (Sa; Grotius) "to perform," (Chaldean; Montanus) or "by doing." God must surely approve of our conduct, since we are unmolested.
But the Vulgate seems preferable, and the people attribute their success to idols, Jeremias 44:17., and 2 Paralipomenon 28:23.

7:11.text: Robbers. Our Saviour alludes to this, Matthew 21:13.
Seen. Think not to hide yourselves, as in a den. My temple shall not be an asylum for wretches.

7:12.text: The sins of a few priests caused this place to be abandoned, (1 Kings 2:22., and 3:4., and Psalm 77:60.) and shall the crimes of a whole people escape?
It is true, that place and Jerusalem were once sanctified: but the people being criminal, God retires.

7:13.text: Early. Sparing no opportunity of reclaiming you.

7:15.text: Ephraim. The ten tribes (Worthington) had been led into captivity in the days of Ezechias, the great-grandfather of Josias.

7:16.text: Praise, offered to appease my wrath.

This admirably shews the force of the saints' intercession, which God does not reject, but rather encourages by such declarations. Abraham and Moses did not leave off praying for the guilty, (Genesis 18:23., and Exodus 32:10.) and the Lord complains that there was none to withhold him, Isaias 64:7., and Ezechiel 22:30.

7:18.text: Heaven. That is, the moon, which they worshipped under that name.
Some understand the sun also and stars, Jeremias 44:17., and Isaias 65:11. All the family contributes to promote this idolatry.

7:21.text: Flesh. I reject your holocausts. Keep them for yourselves.

7:22.text: I commanded them not: viz., such sacrifices as the Jews at this time offered without obedience; which was the thing principally commanded: so that in comparison with it, the offering of the holocausts and sacrifices was of small account.
The observance of the decalogue would suffice. Victims were to teach internal piety, Leviticus 1.
God "brought them out of Egypt, not for the sake of their sacrifices, but that they might....hear his word."
This was the principal design. See Matthew 9:13., and Osee 6:6., and 1 Corinthians 1:17.

7:22.src: St. Thomas Aquinas, [Summa Theologica] 1:2. q. 102. a. 3.
7:22.text: The rules for sacrifices were given after the adoration of the calf, as necessary preservatives against idolatry.
Yet the paschal lamb, etc., had been already commanded.
Many such absolute expressions imply a condition or comparison.
I brought. Several manuscripts read thus more accurately than the Hebrew, "He brought," (Kennicott) which Protestants abandon, (Haydock) though the present reading may stand.
Every variation should be noticed, as this and the former verse "seem greatly corrupted; perhaps the Syriac version will be the best guide to their true meaning."
Yet the passage is sufficiently intelligible in the Vulgate and Septuagint.

7:27.text: Thee. Septuagint omit this verse, which Grabe replaces. The people will be more inexcusable.

7:29.text: High. People lamented in such places, Jeremias 9:13., and Judges 11:35.
Wrath, which he treats with the utmost severity.

7:30.text: Abominations. Hebrew. We have literally, "stumbling-blocks," offendicula.
Manasses placed idols in the very temple, as the last kings of Juda did, 4 Kings 23:4., and Ezechiel 8:3.

7:31.text: Valley, to the south of the city. Here Moloc was adored, and Josias therefore contaminated the place, 4 Kings 23:10.

7:32.text: This was verified when Nabuchodonosor and Titus besieged the city.
The latter was quite shocked. (Josephus, Jewish Wars 6:14.) See Jeremias 19., and 31:40.

7:34.text: Bride. None shall think of marrying, (chap. 16:9., and 25:10.; Calmet) all being filled with lamentation.

8:1.text: Graves. They might suppose that they would find treasures in them, as the tombs of Semiramis, Cyrus, etc., were thus enriched. This inhumanity seems to have taken place before the last siege, Baruch 2:24. (Calmet) Quaeque carent ventis et solibus ossa Quirini Nefas videre dissipabit insoleas.-----
Cruelty and avarice cause persecutors to act thus.

8:4.text: Again. Why then do not the people strive to repent?

8:7.text: Stork. These birds retire at the approach of winter to warmer climates. In Poland swallows plunge into marshes, like frogs. If instinct teach them to do so, for their preservation, is it not strange that men should be so inconsiderate?

8:8.text: Falsehood. The impostors have deceived you, and they are themselves taken prisoners.

8:9.text: Them. Those who understand, and do not observe the law, are not wise.

8:10.text: Others. Idumeans, etc. Yet most part of the land remained uncultivated.
The rest, to ver. 13., "they will collect their fruits, says," etc., is supplied by Grabe.

8:12.text: No, they are too hardened, Jeremias 6:14.

8:13.text: Gather into Jerusalem, to be butchered.
Away. They are now in the greatest distress.
This probably happened a little before the last siege, Jeremias 14.

8:14.text: Silent, in utter destruction, 1 Kings 2:9. We cannot be in a worse condition.

8:16.text: Dan, by which the Chaldeans must pass, Jeremias 4:15. Many have hence inferred that antichrist will be the serpent (ver. 17.) of the tribe of Dan, Genesis 49:17.

8:17.text: Charm. The Chaldeans will not be moved to pity (Menochius) by any words.

8:18.text: Me. God, Jeremias, the people, or rather Jerusalem bewails her children.

8:19.text: Sion. Thus the people complain, (Menochius) or God answers them that they had no reason to have recourse to idols for protection.

8:20.text: Saved. Our hopes of redress are vanished. We must still pine away for want of food.
The siege continues, though we had been assured it would shortly be terminated.

It commenced January 30, the year of the world 3414. Soon after Nabuchodonosor went to meet Pharao, and having defeated him, returned about June, the year of the world 3415, and after 390 days took the city, 27th of July, the year of the world 3416.

8:21.text: The prophet continues to speak in the next chapter.

8:22.text: Galaad was famous for the best, Jeremias 46:11., and Genesis 37:25.
Closed? Have I not sent prophets? But they will not be healed.

9:1.text: "Who will give my head waters, (Haydock) or change it into water, and my eyes into a fountain."
Protestants, "Oh that my head were waters."
The miseries of my people are so great, (Calmet) a few tears would not suffice to bewail them.

9:2.text: Men. Why cannot I retire (Menochius) from this scene of misery, or afford some consolation to the captives? Septuagint, "Who will give me the last station in the desert?"

9:3.text: Lies. They are bent on deceit (Calmet) and murder, (Haydock) ver. 8., and Psalm 63:4., and 119:2.

9:4.text: Deceitfully. A man must guard against his relations and domestics, Micheas 7:5., and Matthew 8:35.

9:5.text: Iniquity. It is become natural to them.
They cannot lay the blame on the violence of passion. They sin on purpose.
Septuagint, "they have not left off, (6.) in order to be converted. Usury upon usury; fraud upon fraud; they," etc.

9:7.text: Try them in the crucible of war, Jeremias 6:27.

9:10.text: Departed. Beasts and birds will not continue long after men cease to cultivate the country.

9:11.text: Sand. The stones were burnt to dust, 2 Esdras 4:2.

9:16.text: Consumed. Jeremias 44:27. No country shall afford them protection.
The richer sort were made captives, and most of the people destroyed, but not all, Jeremias 4:27.

9:17.text: Wise, in composing or singing the Naenia, or mournful songs, recording the praises of the deceased.
"This custom still subsists in Judea: women go about with dishevelled hair and naked breasts, with mournful tunes, exciting all to tears."
Music was also used, Matthew 9:23. Thus feigned tears, at least, would supply the want of real ones.

9:21.text: Streets. Death spares none. The least suspecting fall.
It enters by the windows, if the doors be shut, Joel 2:8.

9:22.text: Speak. Our editions of the Septuagint omit this word, though St. Jerome found it in his copies, (Calmet) and in Theodotion, expressed by "death;" while others rendered it, "speak," as the same letters dbr, may have both meanings, according as people may please to pronounce them.
Grabe supplies, "by death. (22.) These things, says the Lord."

9:24.text: Me. Virtue will save, when riches, etc., will prove useless.
Phocilides, a pagan, said, (Calmet) "Boast not of wisdom, strength, or riches great. One God is wise, and potent too, and rich."

9:26.text: Egypt. the uncircumcised shall be punished as well as Juda, if they transgress. Only the Jewish nation properly observed the right of circumcision generally, or at first. Others imitated them, but with various ceremonies.
Hyrcan obliged the Idumeans to receive circumcision.
Heart. All then became guilty, Romans 2:25.
Neither these nations nor Juda was circumcised in heart, and of course were hypocrites.

10:2.text: Signs. They were designed to point out the seasons, Genesis 1:14.
Heathens thought that they had a divine power.

10:4.text: Asunder. Isaias 44:8., Baruch 6:3., and Wisdom 13:11. The pagans themselves laughed at such folly.
If such things were gods, the makers and tools deserved more adoration: Fabri deorum vel parentes numinum, says Prudentius. The vanity of such idols was more easily discerned than that of the stars.

10:5.text: Tree. The ancient statues were ill-formed, (Diodorus 4.) before Dedalus brought the art to greater perfection, and gave them a living attitude.

10:6.text: There. Septuagint omit the three following verses; and from ver. 9., all these, etc., to the end of ver. 10., which Grabe inserts in a different character.

10:7.text: Wise. Manuscript 2 has in the margin "kings," perhaps more correctly.

10:8.text: Vanity, and shews them clearly to be foolish (Calmet) and wicked.

10:9.text: Ophaz, or Phison, (Genesis 2:11.; Calmet) the coast of Pegu, Faprobana, etc.

10:10.text: Wrath. God is the true cause of all that terrifies mankind.

10:11.text: Heaven. This verse is in Chaldean, for the captives to use at Babylon, to defend themselves. It should be in a parenthesis, as it interrupts the discourse. Perhaps it was added during the captivity.

10:13.text: Voice. Thunder, (Psalm 28:3.) which is usually the forerunner of rain. Lightning is occasioned by the inflamed exhalations of conflicting clouds.

10:14.text: Knowledge. If it were real, he would see the vanity of idols, ver. 8.

10:16.text: Rod, to measure, (Psalm 73:2.) or the sceptre and ruler.

10:17.text: Shame. Idols. See if they will keep thee. Hebrew, "thy merchandise," or most precious effects.

10:18.text: Found by the enemy. Hebrew also, "find" me, being taught by affliction, Jeremias 29:13.
Entering into sentiments of penance, they shall say, Woe, etc.

10:22.text: North. He has announced this conqueror eight times.
Dragons. Tannim means "huge reptiles," whether of sea or land.

10:23.text: The way, etc. Notwithstanding man's free-will, yet he can do no good without God's help, nor evil without his permission. So that, in the present case, all the evils which Nabuchodonosor was about to bring upon Jerusalem, could not have come but by the will of God.
This prince succeeds by thy order.
Yet chastise us as a father, and leave us not to his fury, ver. 24.
"Let new preachers blush, who say that each one is governed by his own will," (St. Jerome; Jeremias 9:23.) and able to do good without God's grace.

10:24.text: Judgment. Chap 30:11., and Psalm 6:1. Justice is not contrary to mercy.

10:25.text: Glory. This is repeated from Psalm 78:6.

The prophet supposes that these nations will not repent, and that their sins are more enormous. To see God's people treated with greater severity, might scandalize the weak, while infidels would take occasion to abuse their own impunity.

11:1.text: The, etc. He continues his discourse, (ver. 15.) or rather addresses the people, when Josias renewed the covenant, 2 Paralipomenon 34:29., Leviticus xxvi., and Deuteronomy xxvii.

11:4.text: Fathers. God chooses his people for obedience, not for kindred, etc.

11:6.text: Cities, or to those who come thence to the temple, that all may know.

11:7.text: For. This and the next verse have not been read by Septuagint; (Calmet) but only "and they did not comply." Grabe supplies the rest.

11:8.text: Brought; or "will bring," seems preferable. What does the history of God's people till the captivity exhibit, but scenes of disorder and of succeeding misery?

11:9.text: Conspiracy. They are leagued together to do evil, with all earnestness.

11:10.text: To them, for a long time. Yet they must not despair, or have recourse to idols, (ver. 12.) or witches, 1 Kings xxviii.

11:13.text: Confusion. Thus he contemptuously denotes Baalim, Jeremias 3:24.
Various idols were objects of adoration, (Haydock) so that no city or street, perhaps not any house, was pure, Jeremias 7:18., and 44:17., Isaias 65:11., and Ezechiel 16:25.

11:15.text: Beloved, dilectus. Hebrew and Septuagint have the feminine. Chaldean, "people." They presumptuously thought that they would be freed from all their abominations, by partaking of the sacrifices.
But the offerings of such are not accepted.
The flesh is holy, but is of no service to the obstinate sinner.

11:16.text: Burnt, by lightning. The olive was a most useful and beautiful tree.

11:18.text: Shewedst me. He insinuates that something more than what appears must be understood.
All Christians explain what follows of Jesus Christ, (St. Jerome; Worthington) whom Jeremias prefigured in his sufferings.
"Let us follow the rule which shews that all the prophets did most things for a type of our Lord and Saviour; and what was now done by Jeremias, foreshewed what would happen to our Lord."

11:19.text: Meek: pet lamb. The Arabs still keep one in their houses, 2 Kings 12:3.
Knew. I acted as if I had been ignorant.
Yet Christ foretold his sufferings, Matthew 20:18., etc.
Bread. Christ, the bread of life, was nailed to the disgraceful wood.
They threaten to beat him, (De Dieu) or to mix a poisonous wood with his food.
Some Jews had corrupted this text in St. Justin's time.

11:20.text: Sabaoth. That is, of hosts or armies; a name frequently given to God, in the Scriptures.
Thy revenge. This was rather a prediction of what was to happen, with an approbation of the divine justice, than an imprecation.
He speaks of the impenitent, (St. Jerome) and wishes they may be converted.

11:21.text: Life. What precedes, was spoken concerning the prophet, though it regarded Christ also in a proper but more elevated sense.

11:23.text: Remains. All these priests shall perish, Psalm 36:28.
He speaks of the wicked, as a remnant was still left, Jeremias 4., and Isaias 10.

12:1.text: Is just. Hebrew, "judgment." Septuagint, "I will make my apology to thee." Jeremias had been grievously persecuted by his countrymen: he therefore mentions a subject which has been the source of much perplexity. The success of the wicked is a temptation for weak souls. See Job xxi., Psalm 72:3., and Habacuc 1:13.
The prophets often speak in their names, not being ignorant or doubtful of the justice of divine providence.

12:2.text: Reins. They have thy name in their mouth, but not in their heart, Isaias 29:13., and Matthew 15:8.

12:3.text: Gather. God orders his prophet to announce the misery of the wicked.

12:4.text: Mourn? Is not this a proof of my providence in chastising Juda? This famine is specified [in] Jeremias 8:13., and 14:4.
Last end. Two letters seem to be transposed in Hebrew, as the Septuagint read "ways."
The impious blaspheme as if all were ruled by chance.

12:5.text: Horses. If thou hast been terrified at the threats of thy fellow-citizens, how wilt thou withstand those of Jerusalem? (Menochius) or he speaks to Juda, who would not be able to resist the Chaldeans, since the Philistines, etc., had routed the Jews, though destitute of cavalry.
Jordan, or the nations of Ammon, etc., on that side.
Protestants, "if in the land of peace, wherein thou trustedst, they wearied thee, then how wilt," etc.

12:6.text: To thee. Edom, etc., made a league with Sedecias, which they broke. They always bore a hatred to the Jews, though sprung from the same stock, Jeremias 27:1., and Lamentations 4:21.

12:7.text: Soul; people, (Haydock) whose punishment was decreed. Say not, therefore, that the wicked always prosper.

12:9.text: As a. Septuagint, "a den of the hyena:" Alexandrian, "robbers." Grabe inserts, "or a den around it?"

They have read áuss, "a den," instead of hait, "a bird." Bochart, (III. 11.) however, shews that the latter word denotes various wild beasts, and nearly follows the Septuagint. Though the inheritance be most charming, it shall not pass unpunished.

12:10.text: Pastors; princes of my people, as well (Haydock) as of the Chaldeans, Jeremias 6:3.

12:11.text: Heart, to seek God and the cause of their misfortunes.

12:13.text: Fruits. They shall not satisfy your expectations or wants.

12:14.text: Land. Nabuchodonosor led the Ammonites, etc., into captivity, five years after the Jews.
After the children are chastised, the rod is burnt.

12:15.text: Land; the Jews, as well as their neighbours, Jeremias 49:6.

12:16.text: People. After the captivity, converts were more cherished. Yet the predictions concerning the call of the Gentiles were accomplished only by the preaching of the gospel.

13:1.text: It denoted the Jews, who became more corrupt during the captivity; (ver. 7, 11.) so that God delivered them out of his pure mercy. Thus He explains this prophetical action.
Water. He made choice of them when they were rough and deformed.

13:4.text: Euphrates, above 150 leagues distant. Some think this was only to be done in spirit: but it is generally supposed that the prophet made this long journey twice. Bochart suggests that Euphrates (Hebrew Peratha) may be put for Ephrata, or Bethlehem, as the first syllable is often cut off.

Yet a journey to the Euphrates would represent more strikingly the destination of the people; and it would not be difficult for God to convey Jeremias thither in a short time, if requisite, as he sent Habacuc through the air to carry a dinner to Daniel; (xiv. 32.) though this supposition is not here necessary, as the labour to which Jeremias was repeatedly exposed, might shew the people their manifold transgressions and captivities.
Such prophetic actions make a deeper impression than words, Hebrews 1:The ingratitude of the people is here described, to Jeremias 21.

13:9.text: Rot. I will bring them low.
They shall see that all their dependance must be on the divine mercy.

13:11.text: Glory; that they might serve me for the edification of the world.

13:12.text: This is another prophetical similitude denoting extreme perplexity.
Do. They deride the prophet, as if he said nothing worthy of notice.

13:16.text: Mountains, beyond the Euphrates, resembling clouds.

13:17.text: My soul. Jeremias can do no more.

13:18.text: Queen; Nohesta, mother of Joachim, or to the consort of Sedecias.

13:19.text: South, belonging to Juda; (Calmet) or Egypt will afford no protection.

13:20.text: Hebrew and Septuagint, "see those who come."
Cattle. O king, what account wilt thou give of the people to the Almighty?

13:21.text: Head. Achaz called the Assyrians into the country, and Ezechias had shewn his treasures to the Babylonians. Other kings excited God's indignation.

13:22.text: Defiled. Thou art exposed to the brutality of the soldiers.

13:23.text: Evil. Bad habits are a sort of second nature. I speak in vain.
Yet God sometimes converts inveterate sinners, who cannot rise of themselves.

13:26.text: Appeared. Thou art treated as a wretched and vile captive.

14:1.text: Drought, during the last siege, (ver. 18.; St. Jerome) or after the captivity of Jechonias; (chap. 8:13.) though the famine might happen in the days of Josias, Joel 1:
This scourge was in punishment of sin, and signified the privation of grace.

14:3.text: Water. Siloe was the only spring near, and it was often dry, (St. Jerome) though it was formerly abundant, (Josephus, Jewish Wars 6:6.) as well as the fountain of Gehon. The city was furnished with cisterns.

14:5.text: Field. They prefer places frequented by men.

14:6.text: Failed, through want, though they be very piercing.

14:7.text: Sake. We have the honour to be called thy people. We urge no merit of our own.

14:8.text: Lodge. The Fathers apply this to our Saviour, in a spiritual sense.

14:9.text: Wandering. Septuagint, "sleeping;" (Haydock) or as a boaster, who thinks himself strong.
Upon us. Shall strangers rule over thy people?

14:10.text: Rested. They are inconstant, fond of novelties, and apply to idols.

14:11.text: Good, that the drought may be removed. (Calmet) See Jeremias 7:16., and 11:14.
"It is folly to pray for him who has sinned unto death," (St. Jerome; 1 John 5:16.) by final impenitence.
Sacrifices will not benefit the impenitent.

14:13.text: Ah. Hebrew has only one exclamation, or three letters, which Aquila renders, Ah, ah, ah., Jeremias 1:6., and Ezechiel 4:14.
Jeremias lays the blame on false prophets.
Yet they afford no excuse to thy followers. Both fall into the ditch.

14:17.text: My. Septuagint, "your eyes." Jeremias shews by his tears the future misery.
Virgin. Though many were sinners, the Church had some just souls.

14:18.text: Famine. Under Jechonias many were slain, and the chiefs carried into captivity, 4 Kings 24:12.
Into. Hebrew, "through the land, and are ignorant." Chaldean, "they apply to their business, to earthly concerns, and care not," Jeremias 5:31.

14:21.text: Glory; heaven, the temple or Jerusalem. How will infidels blaspheme!

14:22.text: Rain. Let not the people have recourse to idols, (Calmet) in despair.

15:1.text: If. The Geneva Bible reads corruptly, "Though Moses...stood," contrary to Hebrew and Greek.
And Samuel. These two had shewn a particular love for the people, Exodus xxxii., and 2 Kings xii.

Ezechiel (xiv. 4.) specifies Noe[Noah], Daniel, and Job, who were eminent for sanctity. Daniel was still alive. Yet God will not grant their request; and he forbids his prophet to pray for those who were resolved not to repent, Jeremias 14:11.

Their punishment was fixed, and God will not remit it at the request either of the living or of the dead. Hence it is evident, that the dead could and did sometimes make intercession, otherwise they would not here be mentioned. To evade this argument, Protestants in the Geneva Bible, suppose God's "meaning to be, that if there were any man living, moved with so great zeal towards the people as were these two, yet he would not grant their request, for so much as he had determined the contrary." Yet surely Jeremias, Daniel, etc., had a similar zeal; and therefore the text speaks of Moses and Samuel in a state of happiness, where their charity is greater than in this life, as St. Jerome, St. Chrysostom (hom. 1. in 1 Thessalonians) and St. Gregory (Mor. 9:12.) explain it.

Jeremias had been praying earnestly for the people in the temple. But God answers his request with a severity rarely witnessed in Scripture, ordering him to drive the people out, or to announce that they should be thus treated.

15:2.text: Death. This Hebrew phrase intimates that some should die by sickness, etc.

15:3.text: Kinds of persecutors, the sword, etc.

15:4.text: Septuagint, "necessities." Protestants, "to be removed into all," etc. (Haydock) This the event verified, Isaias 11:11.
Manasses. Though he obtained pardon, the people would not imitate his repentance. A dreadful warning for princes!
"The people generally perish by the fault of their governors."

15:5.text: "The creatures cannot be so clement as the Creator."

15:6.text: Intreating thee. Hebrew, "of being intreated;" (St. Jerome) or, "of repenting," and suspending the effects of my wrath.
Septuagint, "I will no longer spare them."

15:7.text: Will, or "have scattered" the ten tribes, and many of Juda, before the last siege.

15:8.text: Man, or "a spoiler of the young man," (Haydock) so that the widow shall also be deprived of her children. Hebrew, "the young spoiler," Nabuchodonosor. He shall not come like a night thief, to attack the metropolis.

15:9.text: Seven: many. It is not common for a mother to lose so numerous a family; yet such shall be the misfortune of Jewish parents. The synagogues shall decrease.
Day. Her prosperity shall end on a sudden.

15:10.text: Strife. Jeremias was under continual persecution, (Menochius) yet ceased not to reprimand the wicked.
Usury. Such people are exposed to contention.

15:11.text: Remnant; posterity, (Calmet) or rather possessions, as the prophet never married, Jeremias 16:2.
God assures him that he shall be protected.
The Chaldeans treated him with respect, Jeremias 39:2., and 40:1. Sedecias and the people had often recourse to him. Chaldean, "They will come to intreat thee, when the enemy shall straiten them."

15:12.text: Iron. Shall the iron (that is, the strength of Juda) stand against the stronger iron of the north, (that is, of Babylon) or enter into an alliance upon equal footing with it? No, certainly; but it must be broken by it.

Iron is not easily united with brass, though Pliny ([Natural History?] 34:4.) mentions a statue of this nature. Neither iron nor brass can stand against steel, which is meant by the iron from the north. The Jews shall not hurt Jeremias, ver. 20., and Jeremias 1:17.
Protestants, "shall iron break the northern iron and the steel?" The riches of Juda shall become a prey, ver. 13.
Stronger kingdoms easily oppress their weak allies.

15:14.text: Bring. Septuagint, "enslave thee to thy," etc. Chaldean, "thou shalt serve."
They have read (Calmet) hahabadti. Hebrew has r instead of d, "I will make thee to pass with thine enemies into a," etc.

15:15.text: Patience. That is, let not thy patience and long suffering, which thou usest towards sinners, keep thee from making haste to my assistance.
He is actuated by a zeal for God's glory.
He is afraid of his own weakness, and begs to be delivered shortly, Psalm 12:2.

15:16.text: Eat them. I received them most cordially, (Ezechiel 3:1.; Haydock) as one who is hungry.
Upon me. I was recognized as the prophet of the Lord.

15:17.text: Jesters, who deride religion, (Psalm 1:1.) or sport away their time.
Hand, through vanity. Literally, "and I boast of."
I rejoiced in suffering, as this prophet. Many (Calmet) supply nor from the former part of the sentence.
Threats. I could not refrain from admonishing the people of their ways, (Haydock) which I would not countenance by my presence.
All indeed kept at a distance from me.

15:18.text: Trusted. It is not cured as soon as might be expected.
It continually breaks out again, (Menochius) like a bog not properly drained.
Hebrew, "Thou art become," etc. Chaldean, "break not thy promise, as," etc., Do not reject me when I stand most in need of redress.

15:19.text: Converted, and cease to fear the people; (Calmet) and confide in my precious words, (Haydock) despising the vile threats of the people.
Mouth; interpreter, Exodus 4:16. Chaldean, "If thou wilt recall the wicked into the way of justice, though shalt fulfill my will." See St. Jerome in Psalm 105:32.
To thee. Thus Sedecias and the people acted, Jeremias 21:1., and 42:2.
The prophet must not give way to sinners, but they must be reclaimed.

15:21.text: Mighty; Chaldeans. Providence watched over Jeremias, amid all his enemies.

16:2.text: Place. It was going to be destroyed, and the cares of a family might interrupt the prophet. The Fathers believe that Jeremias never married, (see St. Jerome in Jeremias 23. Isidor.) which was then a rare example.
He always lived a single life, and not only in time of tribulation.

The Church enforces this law of God for her sacred ministers, in subdeacons and the higher orders. St. Paul shews the propriety of such a regulation, (1 Corinthians vii.) which innovators deem unnatural and tyrannical. How then could God enforce it once? With his grace we may observe celibacy like Jeremias.

16:4.text: Earth. The privation of sepulture was accounted one of the greatest evils.

16:5.text: Feasting: usual at burials for all relations.
This custom reduced many to poverty.
Sed caenam funerishaeres Negliget iratus quod rem curtaverit.
Mourn, if thou shouldst meet a funeral, Luke 7:32.

16:6.text: Cut. Moses prohibited this being done in honour of Adonis, Leviticus 19:27., Deuteronomy 14:1., and Ezechiel 27:31. "The custom of cutting the arms and hair still subsists in Judea," says St. Jerome; and in other countries.

16:7.src: Buxtorf, Syn. xxxv.; Proverbs 31:6., Tobias 4:18., and John 11:19.
16:7.text: Cup. Most exquisite things were sent on such occasions.

16:8.text: Feasting. Hebrew, "of drinking," in times of joy.

16:12.text: Fathers. Hence you are justly partakers of their punishment, Matthew 23:32.
A continuance in sin brings on severe punishments; after which, God again shews mercy.

16:13.text: Gods. Elohim, "masters," etc., Jeremias 17:4. Chaldean, "idolatrous nations."

16:15.text: Fathers. He joins consolation with distressing predictions, and alludes to the redemption of mankind, of which the return of the Jews was the most striking pledge.

16:16.text: Rocks. They shall be found in all their lurking places, Ezechiel 12:12., and Habacuc 1:4.
The Medes and Persians shall grant them liberty; or rather Nabuchodonosor shall fish at first, and afterwards hunt or destroy more of the Jews.
Apostolical men (Worthington) shall be like rocks, (St. Jerome) for the protection of their hearers.

16:18.text: Carcasses. So he styles the victims, or idols, the representations of dead men.

16:19.text: Them. After the captivity many were converted, Esther viii., and 1 Esdras 6:21. Edom was forced to receive circumcision. But this was nothing in comparison with the crowds which embraced the gospel.

16:20.text: Make gods. This consideration alone suffices to shew their absurdity.
"Man must now be merciful to god!"
No one can make even a man, much less a god.

16:21.text: Power, in taking vengeance on idolatry.

17:1.text: The. Grabe (de Vit. p. 8.) observes that the four first verses were omitted in the Septuagint by some careless transcriber, long before the days of Origen, who restored them from the Hebrew and the other Greek versions. On such occasions his work was very useful; but the marks being soon neglected, great confusion ensued.
Grabe has restored these verses in his edition.

Eusebius (Dem. x.) and Theodoret acknowledged them; and Nobilius found the first verse thus expressed in many copies, (Calmet) as it is in Grabe: "The sin of Juda is expressed in writing with a finger nail of adamant, cleaving to the breast of," etc.
Altars, to appear more conspicuously to the latest times. This excited God's indignation.
These figurative expressions shew the inveterate malice of the people.

17:4.text: Be left. Protestants, "discontinue from thine," etc.
The land shall have its sabbaths, which thou wouldst not allow.

17:5.text: Thus. Septuagint continue from the last chapter, "cursed," etc.
Sedecias had formed alliances with several princes, instead of turning to the Lord, Jeremias 27., and xxxvii.
Our chief dependence must be on God, not on human policy.

17:6.text: Tamaric. A barren shrub, that grows in the driest parts of the wilderness.
Harhar denotes some sort (Haydock) of "useless wood." (Symmachus) See Jeremias 48:6., and Isaias 17:2.
Salt, like the environs of Sodom, the fruits of which were bad.

17:8.text: Fruit. See Psalm 1:3. (Pindar, Nem. viii.) How different from the wicked!

17:9.text: Unsearchable. Septuagint, "man, who shall know him?"
God alone can search the heart by his own power. He enables saints to do it by the light of glory, or of prophecy; as Eliseus and St. Peter knew secret transactions.

17:11.text: Partridge. Hebrew kore, (Haydock) may mean the "coucou." The kra lays many eggs, all of which she does not hatch.
Protestants, "As the partridge sitteth on eggs, and hatcheth them not," etc.
It is rare that all produce birds; so the unjust do not succeed (Calmet) in the end.

17:12.text: Throne. The temple. We may also read "O high," etc., Jeremias 14:21.

17:13.text: Earth, where alone their portion shall be, (Apocalypse 3:5,) or they shall be forgotten. It is a proverbial expression, like that of Catullus: Dixit: sed mulier cupido quod dixit amanti In vento et rapida scribere oportet aqua.
Living, which never fail, unlike those of the wicked, Jeremias 2:13.

17:15.text: Come. We have several instances of such impious sarcasms, Isaias 28:10., and 34:13., Amos 2:12., and Micheas 2:6.

17:16.text: Troubled. Hebrew, "in haste to be a pastor after thee." Chaldean, "I delay not, at thy command, to announce thy truths to them," Jeremias 1:6. Jeremias was aware of the dangers attending his office, yet not dismayed.
Day: approbation, (1 Corinthians 4:3.) or advantages of the world.
Of man. Hebrew also "of the wretched:" anush. I wished rather for the people's welfare, (Haydock) and sought not their ruin.

17:18.text: Let them be confounded, etc. Such expressions as these in the writings of the prophets, are not to be understood as imprecations proceeding from malice or desire of revenge, but as prophetic predictions of evils that were about to fall upon impenitent sinners, and approbations of the ways of the divine justice.
The prophet has already prevented this objection, ver. 16.

17:19.text: Gate of the palace, or that by which the kings entered the temple, on the west. The time when this admonition was given is not ascertained.

17:21.text: Souls. Those who broke the sabbath were to die, Exodus 30:15., and Numbers 15:32.
The Jews trifle in deciding what is a burden.
Sabbath often denotes the observance of the whole law.

17:25.text: David. I will observe my covenant, 2 Kings 7:12.
Horses: in prosperity, (Menochius) provided this law, as well as all the rest, be observed.
Such temporal rewards were commonly proposed under the law, as spiritual ones are now.

17:26.text: Sacrifices. Hebrew mincha, (Haydock) "offerings" of flour, wine, etc.

17:27.text: The city shall be entirely destroyed.

18:2.text: Potter's. Thus God would shew his dominion over all, Romans 9:21.

18:3.text: Wheel. Hebrew, "stones." Septuagint, "seats;" or "wheel." (Chaldean) (Exodus 1:16.) See Ecclesiasticus 38:32.

18:4.text: How should this idea humble man!
A potter may mould afresh the clay as long as it is soft, but God can change the hardest heart.

18:8.text: Shall repent, having free-will, though prevented by grace.

18:12.text: Hopes. Jeremias 2:25. Septuagint, "we are valiant men."

18:13.text: Virgin. The disorders of Israel are thus enhanced.

18:14.text: Snow. It continues on Libanus seven or eight months, (La Roque quoted. Univ. Hist. 2:p. 363) or all the year; (Tacitus, Hist. 5:6.) and hence the mountain is justly denominated "white," as the Alps and Albion are from albon.

Away. Yet my people abandon me, to serve idols! Hebrew may have many other senses to the same purpose. Septuagint, "Shall the mountain springs fail, or snow from Libanus? Shall a stream forcibly driven by the wind, turn aside?"
The laws of nature are observed by inanimate things, and shall my people be so foolish as to follow mere vanity.

18:15.text: Ancient; followed by the patriarchs, Jeremias 6:16.

18:16.text: Head, through contempt (Calmet) and pity, Lamentations 2:15.

18:17.text: Burning. Hebrew kadim, "eastern," (Haydock) coming from Desert Arabia, Exodus 10:13.
Back. Thus the Lord now treats the synagogue.

18:18.text: Prophet. Jeremias will not cease to upbraid us with our transgressions; or we have guides as good as him, and we shall not be left destitute, as he would intimate.
Tongue, detraction; or make him suffer for what he says. In all the transactions of this prophet, Christ was foreshewn; (Calmet) and here, particularly, the Jews demand the crucifixion.

18:20.text: Remember, etc. This is spoken in the person of Christ, persecuted by the Jews, and prophetically denouncing the evils that should fall upon them in punishment of their crimes.
Jeremias had prayed earnestly for the people, Jeremias 14:17.

18:21.text: Therefore. He foretells of the event, and approves of the chastisement of the impenitent, (Calmet) whose "impurity might be a bad precedent for others.["] (St. Jerome) See Jeremias 17:18.

18:22.text: Robber. So Nabuchodonosor and all professional conquerors are styled.

18:23.text: Destroy. Hebrew, "with them." Use them as vessels of ignominy: abutere.

19:1.text: Priests. They were to furnish the vessel, and accompany the prophets, ver. 10. Septuagint, "and thou shalt take some of," etc.
Words and actions together instruct most powerfully.

19:2.text: Gate, which must have been to the south-east of Jerusalem, leading to the valley.
Here the common people were buried, after the reign of Josias, who contaminated the place where the idol Moloc had been adored.

19:3.text: Tingle, as if he had heard a sudden noise or clap of thunder.

19:4.text: I have no farther regard for it.
Innocents: their own children, ver. 5., and Jeremias 7:31.

19:5.text: Mind. Perhaps the people stupidly thought thus to honour him; but God pointedly condemns such sacrifices.

19:6.text: Valley. Septuagint, "burial-place of slaughter," ver. 2. Here the Chaldeans slew many.

19:9.text: Sons, to punish them for their cruel sacrifices.
This was verified under Sedecias, Lamentations 2:10., and 4:10., and Ezechiel 5:10.

19:11.text: Again. The captives indeed returned, so that this must not be urged too much.
Yet the Romans strictly fulfilled this prophecy, (St. Jerome; Sanctius) and the city was built in another place, and styled Aelia.
God can perform what is impossible to man, Jeremias 18:3., and Matthew xix.

19:13.text: Topheth. This sentence was not in St. Jerome's Hebrew copy, but in the Septuagint (Calmet) with some variations: (Haydock) "All the houses of the kings of Juda, like the place of Topheth," is added by the Septuagint.
In Grabe they agree with the present Hebrew and Chaldean only rendering, "shall be as a place in ruin, for the defilements in all," etc.

Of heaven: the moon, etc., 4 Kings 23:12. Josias could not entirely root out this impiety, Jeremias 32:29. His wicked son Joachaz or Sellum, was probably now on the throne, when the prophet went to the potter, etc., Jeremias 18 -- Jeremias 21.

19:14.text: People. Many still resorted to the temple.

20:1.text: Son, or descendant. His father was Melchia, Jeremias 21:1., and 1 Paralipomenon 9:12.
Chief: high priest, (Theodoret) or rather a chief officer, (chap. 29:25.) or prince, (Matthew 26:27.; Tolet.; Grotius) whose duty it was to take up impostors. He treated Jeremias in this light.

20:3.text: Phassur. This name signifies, increase and principality; and therefore is here changed to Magor-Missabib, or "fear on every side," to denote the evils that should come upon him in punishment of his opposing the word of God.
Aquila renders Posseur, "a stranger," and St. Jerome, "blackness of visage."
It may also mean, "one who causes paleness."
Mogur may signify "fear or distress." Septuagint and Syriac, "an exile."
He deserved to be thus treated, ver. 6.
He would be terrified by many enemies.

20:6.text: Lie. He was therefore a false prophet, and vexed that Jeremias should contradict him.

20:7.text: Thou hast deceived, etc. The meaning of the prophet is not to charge God with any untruth; but what he calls deceiving, was only the concealing from him, when he accepted of the prophetical commission, the greatness of the evils which the execution of that commission was to bring upon him.
Hebrew, "thou hast enticed me," when I declined the office.
God never promised that he should suffer no persecution.
Jeremias might also have supposed that he was to be sent to the Gentiles, Jeremias 1:5.
The oriental languages are much more lofty than ours, and express common things in the strongest manner.

We may perceive the different emotions of fear and joy (Du Hamel) with which the prophet was actuated, like St. Paul, and our Saviour himself. The saints evince the weakness of man and the power of divine grace.
Hebrew, "If thou, Lord, hast deceived me, I am," etc.

20:8.text: Day. They keep asking where are these enemies from the north, the plagues? etc.
He is sorry to see the word of God despised, (Theodoret) and is guilty of a venial pusillanimity, concluding that his words had no good effect.

20:9.text: And there, or "for," etc. I was grieved continually.
I could not however refrain from speaking, Acts 17:16., and 1 Corinthians 9:16., and Job 32:18.

20:10.text: Side, seeking an opportunity to ruin me, as the Pharisees did our Saviour, Psalm 40:10.
Protestants, "familiars watched for my halting, saying, peradventure he," etc.

20:12.text: Let me see, etc. This prayer proceeded not from hatred or ill-will, but zeal of justice.
He expresses in a human manner a future punishment.

20:13.text: Sing. God having shewn that his prayer should be heard, he gives thanks, (Calmet) and thus shews that what he is going to say proceeds not from impatience.

20:14.text: Cursed, etc. In these and the following words of the prophet, there is a certain figure of speech to express with more energy the greatness of the evils to which his birth had exposed him.
The wicked would deem the day of his birth cursed, or unlucky.
Jeremias was now in prison, (Grotius) and people in pain express themselves forcibly, particularly in the East, ver. 7., and Job 3:2.
Perhaps no man had announced the tidings of his birth, or he might be no longer living to feel the effects of a curse: as the day was certainly irrevocably past.

20:16.text: Repented, his decree for the ruin of Sodom being fixed.
Noon. This is more extraordinary than at midnight. Let him always be terrified with dismal sounds.

20:17.text: Who. Septuagint, "because he (the Lord.; Du Hamel; Tirinus) slew me not in my mother's womb."
Syriac, Grotius, etc., explain the Hebrew in the same sense, though it may also agree with the Vulgate, from the womb, or as soon as I was born. O that I had never seen the light!
He abstracts from the effects of original sin.
It is better not to exist than to be in constant misery, Matthew xxvi.
The prophet bewailed the abuse which was made of God's word, by unbelievers, ver. 8.

21:1.text: Sent, after Nabuchodonosor had returned from Egypt. (The year of the world 3415.) This consultation should be placed after Jeremias 37.
Phassur, specified above, and Jeremias 38:1., (Calmet) or a different person.
Sophonias, the second priest, (chap. 37:3., and 52:24.) of the 24th class, (1 Paralipomenon 24:18.) who was slain at Reblatha.
Jeremias threatens the great ones to Jeremias 29. and is persecuted. He spoke this when Nabuchodonosor invaded the country.

21:4.text: City. The people shall turn their arms against each other; or rather the Chaldeans shall use their weapons to destroy them.

21:6.text: Pestilence. Septuagint, "death;" the sword, and various diseases.

21:7.text: To them. This was verified, Jeremias 52., and 4 Kings xxiv.

21:8.text: Death; the former if they submit, the latter if they fight, ver. 9.
God's grace is ever ready, that sinners may be converted.

21:9.text: Spoil. He shall be happy to escape naked, Jeremias 38:3.

21:13.text: Valley. He speaks to Jerusalem, confiding in the strength of her situation, upon rocks, surrounded with a deep valley.
Literally, "valley, solid and in a plain."
Isaias (xxii. 1.) styles it ironically, "the valley of vision."
Jerusalem was situated on many rocks.

21:14.text: Doings. Hebrew, "studies." So "Your studious pursuits" occurs, ver. 12, in many Hebrew manuscripts, though the printed copy have, "their;" and "spoiled" for apprised.
Forest, the temple, which is called Libanus, (Zacharias 11:21.) and the houses built of cedar-wood, (4 Kings 25:9.; Calmet) particularly the royal palace of the forest, 3 Kings vii.

22:1.text: Go. The contents of this chapter are of a more ancient date than those of the foregoing chapter; for the order of time is not always observed in the writings of the prophets.
King Joakim, who succeeded Sellum, the year of the world 3394.

22:5.text: Myself, having none greater, Hebrews 6:13. Oaths evince the insincerity of men, who distrust each other; but when God condescends to use them, it shews men's incredulity.
House, the temple, or rather the palace, where the king was sitting at the gate, ver. 2.

22:6.text: Galaad, the head of Libanus. By Galaad, a rich and fruitful country, is here signified the royal palace of the kings of the house of David; by Libanus, a high mountain abounding in cedar-trees, the populous city of Jerusalem.
Yet the proper Galaad was at some distance, and on the side of Ammon very barren. The palaces had arisen from a miserable state to resemble Libanus. They shall surely be destroyed.
The advantageous situation of Jerusalem should not protect it, no more than it had done Galaad, 4 Kings 15:29.

22:7.text: Prepare. Literally, sanctify; (Challoner) as various religious ceremonies were used before the declaration of war, and to know what would be the success.
The Chaldeans were destined to execute God's decrees.
Cedars; fine buildings, (ver. 14.; Calmet) and chiefs.

22:10.text: Dead. He means the good king Josias, who by death was taken away, so as not to see the miseries of his country.
Away. Sellum, alias Joachaz, who was carried captive into Egypt, (Challoner) ver. 12., and 4 Kings 23:33.
He was the fourth son of Josias, (1 Paralipomenon 3:15.) and had probably the title of king after the death of Sedecias.

22:13.text: Friend. Joakim forced his subjects to work for him for nothing.

22:14.text: Vermilion, (cynopide.) This colour was invented in the town of Pontus, and was used for statues, etc.

22:15.text: Shall thy magnificent palaces secure thy empire?
Him. Obey the Lord, in like manner, that thou mayst prosper.

22:18.text: Sister. They shall not condole with his consort.

22:19.text: Jerusalem. Jeremias 36:30. He died indeed with or like his fathers, 4 Kings 24:6. But it is not said that he was buried.
The Chaldeans designed to send him to Babylon, (2 Paralipomenon 36:6.) but slew him on the road, (Usher, the year of the world 3405) or treated his corpse ignominiously after his arrival.

22:20.text: Go; Jerusalem. The verbs are feminine.
Lovers; citizens, or Egyptians, etc., Jeremias 27:2.

22:22.text: Pastors, or princes, whose expectations of aid will be frustrated, Osee 12:1. Chaldean, "Thy leaders shall be scattered to all the winds."

22:24.text: Jechonias: Joachin, (4 Kings 24:6.; Worthington) or Chenias, who did not take warning by his predecessor's misfortune.
Ring, or seal, if he were most dear to me, the prime minister of the greatest monarch, Aggeus 2:24.
Alexander the Great gave his ring to Perdiccas, designating him his successor, or regent.

22:26.text: Mother; Nohesta, (4 Kings 24:8.) who perverted, (Calmet) or did not correct him in his youth.

22:27.text: Mind; ardently desiring and expecting to be liberated.

22:28.text: Jechonias. Protestants, "Koniah, a despised broken idol? is he," etc. Alexandrian Septuagint, "Jechonias has been dishonoured as a vessel, or which there is no need, for he hath been snatched and cast," etc.

22:30.text: Write this man barren. That is childless: not that he had no children, but that his children should never sit on the throne of Juda, (Challoner) ver. 28., Matthew 1:12., and Ezechiel 17:24. Zorobabel was of his seed, yet he never obtained the title or sovereign power of a king. The Machabees were of a different family.
Joachin, or Jechonias, was restored indeed to some favour, (4 Kings 25:27.) but not to the kingdom, no more than any of his posterity, till Christ.

23:1.text: Pastors. Successors of Josias (Calmet) and false prophets.

23:3.text: Multiplied. The prophets subjoin tidings of peace to such as might cause affliction.

23:4.text: Pastors; Zorobabel, etc., as figures of Christ, and of the pastors of his Church.
No more, as much as formerly. So many expressions must be understood.
Reign. Zorobabel was not chief governor, (Calmet) though at the head of his tribe.
The kingdom of Christ was not of this world, John 18:36.
He alone imparts justice to others.

23:5.text: Pastors; Zorobabel, etc., as figures of Christ, and of the pastors of his Church.
No more, as much as formerly. So many expressions must be understood.
Reign. Zorobabel was not chief governor, (Calmet) though at the head of his tribe.
The kingdom of Christ was not of this world, John 18:36.
He alone imparts justice to others.

23:6.text: Saved: less disturbed. The world shall be redeemed, Luke 2:14., and Ephesians 2:14.

Just. Hebrew, "Justice," by whom we are made just. He is the true God, Colossians 2:9, 14. No man could bear this title: and Grotius insidiously attempts to apply it to the Jews, though the Chaldean, etc., clearly speak of the Messias. He [Grotius] must be read with great caution.
Septuagint, "This is his name, which the Lord will call him, Josedech. In the prophets. (9.) My," etc.

23:8.text: Land. The deliverance from Egypt and Babylon were emblems of man's redemption, which was accomplished by far greater miracles.

23:9.text: Prophets of falsehood. He directs his discourse to them, and afterwards to the priests, being filled with amazement at their impending ruin.

23:10.text: Cursing of the Lord, or the abuse of his sacred name.

23:11.text: Defiled. Hebrew, "hypocrites." My priests retain the appearance of religion, to join with impostors in deluding the people.

23:14.text: Jerusalem. These were far worse, as they pretended to be inspired by God, whereas those of Samaria publicly adhered to Baal; so that the people must have been foolish to have been deceived by them.

23:15.text: Gall, or something mortally bitter, Jeremias 9:15.

23:16.text: Own. To rely on private judgment, and not on the Church, is a mark of falsehood.

23:17.text: Blaspheme. Hebrew, "despise;" or Septuagint, "reject disdainfully the word of the Lord, you," etc., Jeremias 6:14., and 8:11.

23:18.text: Counsel. Hebrew, "secret." Which of you is acquainted with his designs? I will inform you, that your perdition is at hand, ver. 20.

23:21.text: Not send. The want of mission proves a prophet or preacher to be false.

23:22.text: I should. Some copies read avertissent, "they would have turned," conformably with the Hebrew, etc., and we should suspect that this was a mistake of the transcriber, if St. Jerome did not agree with the Vulgate in his commentary, (Calmet) though not in express terms: (Haydock) "I also should not have abandoned them to impurity....Let us behold how heretics, having once given way to despair,...seek the gratification of their sensual appetite."

23:23.text: Off? Can you so easily penetrate my designs, or escape my fury? Am I like your idols of yesterday? Septuagint and Theodotion, "I am a God at hand," etc., which agrees with the sequel.

23:27.text: Dreams, as if God had favoured them with his revelations.
Fathers; the false prophets of Baal, whom these imitate.

23:28.text: Dream, to those who are really sent by God, (Calmet) and recognized, 1 Corinthians 14:29.

23:29.text: Pieces? True prophets will have a zeal fo the conversion of souls, Jeremias 20:9., and Hebrews 4:12.

23:30.text: Neighbour, causing those whom I have sent to be disregarded.

23:31.text: Use. Hebrew also, "sharpen, or render smooth," and insinuating, Psalm 49:16.

23:32.text: Wonders. Protestants, "lightness." Septuagint, "frauds," or pretended miracles.
False prophets cannot work true miracles: but it is more difficult to distinguish these than the former mark of being lawfully sent, ver. 21.

23:33.text: Burden, Massa, (Haydock) denotes a weight, or sometimes a prediction.

23:34.text: Burden of the Lord. This expression is here rejected and disallowed, at least for those times: because it was then used in mockery and contempt by the false prophets, and unbelieving people, who ridiculed the repeated threats of Jeremias under the name of his burdens.

23:35.text: Answered. I will make you alter your language by chastisements.

23:39.text: Take. Hebrew of the Masorets, (Calmet) "I, even I, will utterly forget (Protestants; Haydock) or abandon you." (Chaldean) But the sense of the Septuagint and Vulgate seems preferable.

24:1.text: Year of the World 3405, Year before Christ 599.

24:2.text: Season. Appearing in autumn and ripe in spring,

24:5.text: Jechonias was treated with honour, (4 Kings 25:27.) as well as Daniel and Zorobabel, of the same family. (Daniel 2:48.; Jeremiah 29:5.) The captives, under Sedecias, were much more abused.
This is the literal sense; the mystical shews that the good shall be rewarded.

24:6.text: Up, till the Messias appear, and the true Israel of God, his disciples, to whom the promises chiefly pertain. They will continue for ever. If this answer does not give satisfaction, as it ought, we may say that the Jews did not comply with the condition, and were therefore abandoned to the Romans.

24:8.text: Egypt, to which they fled contrary to the prophet's advice,

25:1.text: Year of the World 3397, Year before Christ 607.

Babylon, when he was associated by Nabopolassar, three years before his death, the year of the world 3397. This year Joakim was taken to be conducted to Babylon, though he was afterwards permitted to remain on very hard conditions, while the sacred vessels, Daniel, etc., were taken away, and the 70 years of captivity commenced. They ended in the first of Cyrus, the year 3468.
This chapter should be placed before the 24th and after the 26th.
The prophets did not observe the order of time, Jeremias 21.

25:3.text: Josias. He prophesied nineteen years under him, and three under his successors.

25:4.text: All. We know of Joel, Habacuc, Sophonias, and Holda.

25:9.text: My servant. So this wicked king is here called; because God made him his instrument in punishing the sins of his people.
He thought himself more than man, but he was only the rod destined for the fire.

25:10.text: Sound, or songs of women turning the mill, Matthew 24:41.
Lamp, or illuminations, on account of some victory, (Haydock) or festival. (Pers. 5:180.) The Jews "light a lamp for the sabbaths," (Seneca, Ep. xcv.) before they commence.

25:11.text: Years. Ver. 1. The neighbouring nations were also involved in this calamity, and were to be sent back by Cyrus.
Another period of 70 years is specified, (chap. 29:10.) during which the city and temple should remain in ruins, till the second of Hystaspes, the year of the world 3485.

This system is not without difficulties. The present prediction seems rather to refer to the desolation, (Vatable; Aggeus 1:2.) as appears from Zacharias 1:12., or the prophet speaks of the same event in both places, dating from the year preceding the capture of Jerusalem, (the year 3415), till Darius gave entire liberty to the Jews, the year 3485. We differ from Usher in the years allotted to Cyrus, who began to restore the Jews, 1 Esdras 1:5.
These 70 years are dated from the 11th of Sedecias.

25:12.text: Cyrus overturned the monarchy, and the city was ruined by degrees, Isaias xiii., and xiv., and 21:1., and Ezechiel 1:5.

25:14.text: Kings. They perfidiously joined the Chaldeans, after making a league with Sedecias, Jeremias 27:3. This is condemned, (Calmet) and not precisely their submitting to Nabuchodonosor, Jeremias 28:8. Their league with Juda was indeed wrong; but the infringement of it was another crime. Septuagint is here much transposed almost to the end. See Grabe.
Jeremias had prophesied against the nations, though his words are given, Jeremias 46.

25:15.text: All, who might be then at Jerusalem. Sanctius thinks Jeremias travelled into all these countries: most believe it was only done in vision. He might write to them.
The cup metaphorically denotes God's wrath, Psalm 74:9.

25:18.text: As, etc. He probably inserted this (Haydock) after the event, (Vatable) or the country was much distressed even under Sedecias.

25:19.text: Pharao, who was defeated (Ezechiel 30:25.) coming to assist Juda, (chap. 37.) and again plundered after the taking of Tyre, Ezechiel 29:18.

25:20.text: In. Hebrew, "the mixed multitude," (Exodus 12:38.; Calmet) or "Arabs."
Azotus, taken by the Egyptians, and afterwards by the Chaldeans, Jeremias 47:2.

25:21.text: Ammon. The details are given in Jeremias 48.; Jeremias 49.; Ezechiel 25. -- Ezechiel 34.; Abdias 1:1 and Sophonias 1:1.

25:22.text: Kings. Ithobaal (Josephus, Antiquities 10:11.) had many governors under him. Nabuchodonosor besieged Tyre for 13 years, Ezechiel 26., etc.
Sea, or Gibraltar. See Jos. sup. 5:22.[Josephus, Antiquities 10:22.?] (Haydock) He had navies on the Mediterranean, Ezechiel 30:9.

25:23.text: Buz. Scenite Arabs, who cut off the hair of the eyebrows, Jeremias 9:26. These Saracens left the hair below the ears long, as the Polonians and Hungarians do.

25:25.text: Zambri, sprung from Cethura, and dwelling in Arabia, (Calmet) or Persia, (St. Jerome) where Pliny ([Natural History?] 6:28.) places the Zamarenians.
Elam. Persians, (Haydock) by the sword of Alexander [the Great?], (St. Jerome) or Cyrus subdued those who were subject to the Medes, and united the two nations.
North. Armenia, etc., subdued by Cyrus and by Alexander.
Brother. When Cyrus stood up for the Persians. All shall drink, as at a feast, (Calmet) of this bitter wine.
Face, and forming the empire of Babylon.
Sesac. That is Babel, or Babylon; which after bringing all these people under her yoke, should quickly fall and be destroyed herself.
The Chaldeans are not expressed, to avoid their resentment. The sh in sheshac, is at the same distance from the end as b in Babel is from the beginning of the alphabet. See St. Jerome.
Yet they are not elsewhere spared, Jeremias 49., etc. Sesac was probably the idol, "anais or the moon."

25:25.src: Dio. Chrys.[St. Chrysostom?] iv.; Strabo xi.; Calmet
25:25.text: The Sacean feasts were very dissolute, like the saturnalia at Rome.

Cyrus took Babylon after he had conquered the rest of Asia, and then seizing Nabonides at Borsippe, which was sacred to Anais, "the moon," (Calmet) or Diana, (Strabo xv.) suffered him to die in peace.
Thus fell the king of Sesac, an idol worshipped both at Borsippe and at Babylon.

25:26.text: Zambri, sprung from Cethura, and dwelling in Arabia, (Calmet) or Persia, (St. Jerome) where Pliny ([Natural History?] 6:28.) places the Zamarenians.
Elam. Persians, (Haydock) by the sword of Alexander [the Great?], (St. Jerome) or Cyrus subdued those who were subject to the Medes, and united the two nations.
North. Armenia, etc., subdued by Cyrus and by Alexander.
Brother. When Cyrus stood up for the Persians. All shall drink, as at a feast, (Calmet) of this bitter wine.
Face, and forming the empire of Babylon.
Sesac. That is Babel, or Babylon; which after bringing all these people under her yoke, should quickly fall and be destroyed herself.
The Chaldeans are not expressed, to avoid their resentment. The sh in sheshac, is at the same distance from the end as b in Babel is from the beginning of the alphabet. See St. Jerome.
Yet they are not elsewhere spared, Jeremias 49., etc. Sesac was probably the idol, "anais or the moon."

25:26.src: Dio. Chrys.[St. Chrysostom?] iv.; Strabo xi.; Calmet
25:26.text: The Sacean feasts were very dissolute, like the saturnalia at Rome.

Cyrus took Babylon after he had conquered the rest of Asia, and then seizing Nabonides at Borsippe, which was sacred to Anais, "the moon," (Calmet) or Diana, (Strabo xv.) suffered him to die in peace.
Thus fell the king of Sesac, an idol worshipped both at Borsippe and at Babylon.

25:29.text: City. Jerusalem first fell a prey.

25:30.text: Beauty. The temple, which was like the palace (Calmet) of the great king.
Grapes. Great feasting was then customary. The soldiers rushing to battle, "answer" the Lord.
People encouraged one another by songs under the labour of the vine-press, as those in distress must do.

25:31.text: Flesh. He will justify his conduct, particularly at the last day.

25:32.text: To nation. Jerusalem, Tyre, Syria, desert Arabia, Ammon, Idumea, and Egypt, shall fall one after another. Thus Cyrus will attack the Medes, Asia, and Babylon.

25:34.text: Leaders. Septuagint, "rams." He addresses the princes.
Vessels. Septuagint, "chosen rams," fattened for slaughter.

25:37.text: Silent. The places where you fed your flocks so delightfully, are laid waste.

25:38.text: The dove. This is commonly understood of Nabuchodonosor, whose military standard, they say, was a dove. But the Hebrew word Jonah, which is here rendered a dove, may also signify a waster or oppressor, which name better agrees to that unmerciful prince; or by comparison, as a dove's flight is the swiftest, so would their destruction come upon them.
Septuagint, "waste or impassible before the great sword,["] chap 4:7.
While God, like a lion, protected his people, none durst invade them.

What is said respecting the Babylonian standards is very dubious, (Grotius; Calmet) as the same expression is applied to the Persians, (chap. 50:16.; Menochius) though it may there also be understood of the Chaldeans.
God is like a dove, yet terrible.

26:1.text: Year of the World 3395, Year before Christ 609.
Beginning, after Joakim had reigned some time, ver. 21.

26:2.text: Word. This example all pastors should follow, in the midst of dangers.

26:3.text: If. God is not ignorant, (Calmet) but he preserves man's free-will.
He threatens conditionally, if people persist in evil.

26:6.text: Curse. No greater punishment could be threatened or endured in this world.

26:8.text: Death. The pretext was plausible, as God seemed to have promised the temple an eternal duration, 3 Kings 9:3. The Jews accused Christ of the like crime, Matthew 26:61. Such questions were brought before the sanhedrim and priests, Jeremias 26:57. But the latter are here (Calmet) the chief accusers with "the false prophets."

The princes, moved by the elders, (ver. 17.) acquit the prophet, (Calmet) at the persuasion of Ahicam, ver. 24. Several priests would probably judge along with the other princes, as the examination of impostors belonged chiefly to their tribunal.
All is often used for most part.

26:15.text: Sent me. This was a complete justification.
None could deny but Jeremias had all the characters of a true prophet, ver. 16.

26:16.text: All. The populace easily changes either for better or for worse.

26:17.text: Ancients. They declare what happened about 100 years before, respecting Micheas 3:12.

26:18.text: Mountain. Sion, on which the house of the Lord was built. Conquerors have sometimes ploughed up cities.
Jam seges est ubi Troja fuit.

26:20.text: There was. The adversaries make this reply, or the others contrast the conduct of Joakim with the piety of Ezechias.
