26:23.text: Egypt, as a seditious person....Joakim was tributary to Pharao.

26:24.text: Ahicam. A man employed under Josias, (4 Kings 22:12.) and father of Godolias, who was also the protector of Jeremias, Jeremias 39:14., and 4 Kings 25:22.

27:1.text: Year of the World 3395, Year before Christ 609.
Joakim. This revelation was made to the prophet in the beginning of the reign of Joakim: but the bands were not sent, to the princes here named, before the reign of Sedecias, ver. 3.
He wore them six or seven years.
But this is uncertain. Septuagint omit this title. Syriac, Arabic, etc., read "Sedecias."
As far as Juda may belong to the last chapter. St. Jerome includes the whole verse.
It seems to be rather a fresh prophecy, or the title may be spurious, like many in the Psalms. See Jeremias 26:1., and 28:1., and Isaias 21:13.

27:2.text: Chains. Hebrew, "a yoke and pole," (Calmet) or piece of wood for the necks of slaves.
Thus would the servitude of these nations be clearly represented.
Bands and chains were proper signs, as they were used to bind captives.

27:3.text: Edom. Thus he exercises the function of prophet among the nations, Jeremias 1:5. These had come to compliment Sedecias, or rather to form a league.

27:5.text: Eyes. God disposes of all princes, etc. His prophets speak boldly.

27:6.text: Beasts. The property of those people, or the most barbarous nations.
Hangmen and executioners may in the same sense be styled God's servants.

27:7.text: His son; viz., Evilmerodach, and his son's son, viz., Nabonydus, or Nabonadius, the Baltassar of Daniel, (chap. 5.) and the last of the Chaldean kings.
Come. It will not be long before this rod is cast into the fire, the usual fate of those who execute God's vengeance.

27:9.text: Prophets, who deluded the Jews, as diviners did the Gentiles.

27:10.text: To remove. Hebrew, "that I may," etc. This will be the effect, though contrary to their intention.

27:11.text: In it. None complied, and though the Idumeans, etc., joined the Chaldeans against Juda, they were punished (Calmet) for their former league, ver. 3.

27:16.text: Time. Hananias specified two years, (chap. 28:3.) which proved false. Some "silver vessels," (Baruch 1:8.) and not those of gold, it seems, (Calmet) or not all, (Haydock) were restored at the request of Saraias, Jeremias 51:59.

27:18.text: Hosts. If they prevail, account me a false prophet.
The things taken shall not be soon restored; but more shall be conveyed away.

27:19.text: To thee. Hebrew also, "concerning," as [in] ver. 21., (Haydock) though (Calmet) inanimate things are often spoken to.

28:1.text: Year of the World 3408, Year before Christ 596.

Juda. So far Sanctius joins with the former chapter, improperly. In the 4th year, seems rather an interpolation; though the Septuagint omit in the beginning, etc., to reconcile the passage; and others date the 4th year from the last sabbatical one, which is quite unusual. See Jeremias 27:1.
Sedecias reigned 11 years, so that the 4th might be said to be the beginning.
Prophet. Septuagint, "false prophet," to explain the meaning: the original denotes any prophet.

28:3.text: Years. Some were brought back in the 4th of Sedecias; (chap. 27:16.; Haydock) so that this must be false, whether he spoke in the 1st or 4th year of his reign.

28:6.text: Do. Hebrew also, "will do." He speaks ironically; or shews that he wishes not the misfortune of his countrymen.
He approves of the good thing, but warns his people that it is falsely promised.

28:9.text: Truth. This criterion was given by Moses; and another was assigned in case the prophet attempted to introduce idolatry, which was not here the case, Deuteronomy xiii., and 18:20.

28:12.text: Way, mildly. God inspired him what to say soon after.

28:13.text: Iron. Deuteronomy 28:48. The rashness of Hananias brought a heavier punishment on his admirers, which is the lot of all who follow heresiarchs.

28:17.text: Seventh month. He had spoken in the 5th of the sacred year, ver. 1.

29:1.text: Year of the World 3405, Year before Christ 599.
It was probably sent at the beginning of the reign of Sedecias by his ambassadors, and is different from that sent by Baruch 1. and Jeremias 52:28.

Daniel had begun to prophesy, the year of the world 3402. Ezechiel commenced only five years after the captivity of Jechonias. One prophet frequently instructs another, as Nathan did David, Daniel 9:2., and 1 Peter 1:11.
Jeremias declares that the captivity would continue long. He comforts and rebukes to Jeremias 40.

29:3.text: Gamarias, the son of Saphan, is different. These two are unknown.

29:5.text: Build. Establish yourselves, as you must remain a long time in captivity. We are exhorted not to be attached to the things of the world, to remind us that we shall not live long, Jeremias 16:2., and 35:7., and 1 Corinthians 7:29.

29:7.text: Peace. We must submit to the magistrates, where we reside, Baruch 1:11., and 1 Timothy 2:1.

29:8.text: Prophets. There were many false ones, ver. 21., and Ezechiel xiii.
Dreams. The prophets, or people. They are vain.

29:10.text: Seventy. Dating from the 4th of Joakim to the 1st of Cyrus, the year of the world 3468. (Calmet) See Jeremias. 25:1, 11.

29:11.text: An end. Septuagint, "these things." This version is much abridged. From ver. 15., to 21., is omitted in St. Jerome's and the Roman edition; but not in the Complutensian or Theodoret.
Grabe inserts what is wanting, and places the 15th at the end of our 20th verse.

29:15.text: Said. Expressing your confidence in Providence; or rather, if we join this with what follows, he rebukes them for trusting in false prophets, who represented their condition as worse than that of their brethren, who were left behind; whereas it was really better, ver. 17., and Jeremias 24:2, 8.
The deluded people supposed that the false prophets had been sent by God.

29:19.text: By night, or "early," de nocte, as soon as the night was over.

29:22.text: Curse. Ver. 18. The malediction which has befallen the Jews, "overtake thee."

Fire, in frying-pans, (see 2 Machabees 7:5.) or cauldrons of boiling oil. This death shews that these were not the infamous judges who tempted Susanna, (Calmet) as the Jews quoted by (Haydock) St. Jerome, Salien, etc., would infer from ver. 23., and Daniel 13:57. They were stoned to death by the people, ibid. lxii.[Daniel 13:62.]
Yet the king might order their dead bodies to be cut in two and burnt, to express his horror of their wicked conduct, Daniel 13:55, 59.

29:23.text: Folly. A crime, Judges 19:23., and 2 Kings 13:12.

29:24.text: And. God orders his prophet what to say, to an accusation sent from Babylon, in consequence of the former letter, ver. 5, 28.
Nehelamite, "torrent," (St. Jerome) or "dreamer." We know of no place of this name.

29:25.text: The second priest, (4 Kings 25:18.) a chief officer of the temple, Jeremias 21:1.

29:26.text: Joiada, whose zeal had been conspicuous long before, under Joas, in persuading him to destroy the false prophets, 4 Kings 11:17. Sophonias is exhorted to imitate him, by treating Jeremias in like manner.
Raveth. Hebrew, "is possessed, and counterfeits the prophet." Such were to be slain, being confined till sentence had been passed, Deuteronomy 18:20.

29:28.text: Sophonias read the letter, approving of its contents, (St. Jerome) though this is not certain. He took no violent steps against Jeremias, as Phassur had done.

29:32.text: Sit, in an honourable station, or they shall all die.

30:1.text: Year of the World 3406, Year before Christ 598.

30:2.text: Book. This was spoken in the reign of Sedecias, for the people's conviction. The prophet had received orders to write in the 4th year of Joakim, Jeremias 36:1.

30:3.text: Come. Some in St. Jerome explain this and the following chapter of the end of the world, when all shall confess Christ. Others refer them to the preaching of the gospel alone.
But the return from captivity is specified, as prefiguring that event.
It is probable that many of the ten tribes returned to Samaria, Jeremias 31.; Ezechiel 33.

30:4.text: To Juda. All the race of Abraham are concerned. The kingdom was no longer divided.

30:5.text: We. Jeremias is ordered to express the alarms of the captives, at the news of the destruction of Jerusalem, or rather of the Chaldean empire, by Cyrus. They were naturally afraid that they would also suffer.

30:6.text: Bear. Literally, "beet," generat. But it has here the former signification, (Haydock) pariat.
Yellow. The Babylonians are in great anxiety.

30:7.text: Great, and terrible for this city, the outer walls of which shall be demolished, (Beros.; Calmet) and all its glory perish.
Of it. Cyrus liberated the Jews, 1 Esdras i.

30:8.text: Strangers. Idols. The people were not so prone to worship them. Yet the Jews were almost constantly subject to foreigners (Calmet) despectissima pars servientium, Macedonibus invalidis....sibi ipsis reges imposuere.
Christ granted a more perfect liberty to the faithful, John 8:33.

30:9.text: David. That is, Christ, of the house of David.

Grotius and some modern Jews, in opposition to their ancestors, (Chaldean; Kimchi, etc.) and to all Christians, would understand Zorobabel, though he was never possessed of the title or authority of king.
The prophecy may allude to him, but it is fulfilled only in Christ.

30:11.text: Nations, which are now no more. Grabe supplies ver. 10, 11, 15., and 22.
Judgment, like a father, (Calmet) though the chastisement may seem cruel, ver. 14.
Hebrew, "with justice, but I will not deny thee for ever." Chaldean, "utterly."
Only the Church is preserved continually. All other kingdoms change.

30:13.text: Up. There is none to judge thy cause, or to be thy physician.

30:14.text: Lovers. Nations which had seduced thee to worship their idols.

30:16.text: Prey. The Romans utterly overturned the Macedonian empire, (Haydock) as the former had done the Persian, and they the Chaldean monarchy, which has risen on the ruins of the Assyrian empire. But the Jews rise as it were from their ashes.

30:17.text: Close. Septuagint, "remove the healing plaster from thy painful wound."

30:18.text: Temple. After 70 years, it was rebuilt. The Church was founded on a rock. [Matthew 16:18.]

30:19.text: Lessened. The Jews were as numerous in our Saviour's time (Haydock) as ever they had been.

30:21.text: Leader. Zorobabel, the figure (Theodoret) of Christ, who is here meant.
The sceptre was not taken away till his coming, Genesis xlix.
Who. Cyrus alludes to this passage in his decree, 1 Esdras 1:
The prediction is fully verified in Christians.
Christ is near to God, being one, John xiv.

30:23.text: Wicked, at Jerusalem, or rather the Chaldeans; and the Jews, who crucified their Messias.

31:1.text: Israel. The ten tribes returned as well as Juda, etc.
They were more ready to receive Christ than the other two tribes, Matthew xiii., etc.

31:2.text: Desert. From which the former inhabitants had been driven, (4 Kings 17:6, 24.; Calmet) or, as those under Moses were favoured, (Haydock) so shall the captives.

Septuagint, "I found him warm," (thermon, means also a lupin, which has misled the old Latin interpreters. St. Jerome) murdered "in the desert, with those slain by the sword. Go, and destroy not Israel."

31:3.text: Afar. He has seemed to despise me, (St. Jerome) or he has spoken to my ancestors.
Chaldean, He has "manifested himself long ago to our fathers. Prophet, tell them I have," etc., Psalm 35:11., and 108:11.

31:5.text: Samaria. Its wine was famous, Judges 9:27.
Time, three years being elapsed, Leviticus 19:35.
Protestants, "plant, and shall eat (marginal note, profane) them as common things." They shall not be too greedy, (Haydock) but shall have leisure to enjoy the fruits of their labour.

31:6.text: Watchmen. Some were stationed on eminences to observe the first appearances of the moon, (Calmet) which was a sort of festival.

31:7.text: Save. Hebrew Hoshang, "I beg," was usually added; and this acclamation is used by the Church.

31:8.text: Together. All shall hasten, and there shall be no impediment, Isaias 35:5.

31:9.text: Shall, or "came" into captivity, Baruch 6:6., Psalm 125:6., and Isaias 66:20.
Way. They shall find every accommodation, and plenty of water, Isaias 35:7., and 49:10.
Born. Chaldean, "beloved." I will treat the ten tribes as well as Juda. Christ inebriates his disciples with his graces.
Ephraim shall be treated with all the love and attention shewn to the first-born.

31:12.text: Sion. There shall be no farther schism or idolatry among them.
No more, if they prove faithful. This was not the case.

31:14.text: Fatness. Abundant crops secured them more plentiful tythes[tithes], as long as the people preserved their religion.
No nation had more priests, or better provided for.

31:15.text: There was a city of this name near Bethlehem, where Rachel was buried. Benjamin was her son, and was conducted by this road to Babylon.

Ephraim and Manasses were also her grandchildren, and she may bewail their captivity by personification. St. Matthew 2:18. shews that this prediction was more fully accomplished when the innocents were slain. The same passage may allude to different events.
All the people of God, both of the ten and of the two tribes, bewailed their captivity, and the mothers lamented for those slain near Bethlehem.

31:16.text: Reward. A time fixed for thy tears being dried up, Isaias 16:14., and 40:10.

31:18.text: I was. Chaldean, Theodoret, and St. Chrysostom have "not." But chastisement produced a salutary effect upon the ten tribes, Osee 6:1., and 14:3.
Convert. Bring me back from captivity and from my evil ways. This must be attributed to grace.
God's grace is the principal cause of justification. Man's co-operation is the secondary cause.

31:19.text: Thigh, through grief. Femur moerenti plangere dextra.
Cyrus struck his thigh, mounted his horse, and went to attend the funeral of Abradates.

31:20.text: I spoke. Hebrew, "my word is in him," and he is docile to my instructions.

31:21.text: Tower, to bewail thy past follies; or signals to know the way, or tombs for thy beloved.

31:22.text: Daughter. Bury thy dead, and delay thy return no longer. The Israelites did not soon make use of the leave granted by Cyrus.
The fathers generally explain this of the incarnation.
Christ had the perfect use of reason in the virgin's womb.

He was the brightness of his Father's glory, Hebrew 1:3. Even some ancient Jews apply this to the Messias. (Galatin. 7:14.) But the moderns understand that women would offer themselves in marriage; the times would be so happy. This was not so unusual in countries where polygamy prevailed, Ruth 3:9., Isaias 4:1., and Canticle of Canticles 8:1.
Hence this is hardly the meaning; for God promises something new.
"The Lord hath created a new thing in a woman."

Symmachus agrees nearly with the Septuagint, "because the Lord has created thee by salvation a new plantation. Men shall go about in thy salvation." This "might be explained, if it were not sacrilegious to argue respecting God's word by human sense," says St. Jerome; though this censure of "the Vulgate" (Septuagint) seems rather harsh. He complains here of his copy. Theodoret explains it of the apostles going through the world to spread the gospel.
Behold whence thy happiness must come, (Menochius) O thou who hast so long proved faithless, going astray after many lovers.
Christ, in his mother's womb, was in stature small, but a perfect man (Worthington) in the use of reason, etc.

31:23.text: Bless thee. Thus the captives speak in rapture at their return.

31:24.text: Together. Jerusalem shall be large enough to contain all, at the great festivals.

31:26.text: To me. Thus Jeremias concludes his prediction with joy.

31:27.text: Men. They shall increase exceedingly by my blessing.
Beasts were the source of their riches. Hence pecunia is derived from pecus.

31:29.text: Edge. Such observations had been too often made, Ezechiel 18:12.
Henceforward you shall not suffer for the faults of Achab, Manasses, etc. Each one shall bear his own burden. The captivity has been like a deluge, cleaning all away.

31:31.text: Covenant. That made with the captives was not such. Their covenant is grown old, and at an end, as St. Paul shews, Hebrews 8:8. They were not indeed divided, as they had been, Ezechiel 37:16.

31:32.text: Dominion. As a husband, (Hebrew; Calmet) or "Lord."

31:33.text: Heart. They were more docile after their return: yet still their service was very imperfect and forced. How many false traditions were received, at the coming of Christ! This of course, regards his disciples.

31:34.text: Lord. Christ himself came to instruct mankind. The true God was better known than ever, even by the illiterate. Yet God requires us to have recourse to men, in order to know his truths, as St. Paul was sent to Hananias, and the eunuch to Philip. [Acts viii., and ix.]
The apostles were enlightened by the Holy Ghost, (John 6:45.; St. Augustine, de Spir.; Calmet) who still guides the flock by his pastors. The private spirit is too fanatical and delusive.
The most ignorant shall easily become acquainted with the truths of salvation. External preaching is requisite, though of little use unless grace touch the mind and the heart.

31:34.src: Houbigant, pref. in Prop. 356; Isaias 11:9., and 45:23., and Sophonias 3:9., etc.; Haydock
31:34.text: All will hear successively, (Haydock) or embrace the gospel at the same time, for several years before the last day.

31:36.text: Ever. Like the Chanaanites and Ephraim during the captivity, Isaias 7:8. God restored the latter. Yet they are again dispersed from above 17 centuries, so that this perpetuity, which shall equal the world's duration, belongs to the Church.
Israel remains, not in the incredulous Jews, but in those who with the apostles embraced and propagated the faith of Christ.

31:37.text: Out, experimentally, and not by philosophical hypotheses, which were little known in the days of Jeremias.
This is impossible; so it is that I should abandon Israel.
The Israel of God embraced the gospel.

31:38.text: Come, is only in the margin of the Hebrew, but is found properly in some manuscripts.
Corner. On the south, opposite to the former, 2 Esdras 3:1.

31:40.text: Valley of Hinnom, or Topheth, (chap. 7:32., and 19:6.) where Cedron flowed, Zacharias 14:4.
The city was much enlarged on this side, under the Machabees.

Ever. Yet the temple was utterly destroyed by the Romans, so that we must explain this of the Catholic Church, founded on a rock, and proof against the gates of hell, and all the malice of heretics and persecutors, Matthew 16:18.

32:1.text: Year of the World 3415, Year before Christ 589.
Tenth. The city had been besieged about a year, (chap. 39:1.) and the prophet continued to admonish the king and people of their fate, Jeremias 34.
He bought land, to shew that they should one day return.

32:2.text: Court, at large, so that people might come to him freely. He was not chained or in prison, as

32:4.text: Eyes. They were afterwards put out at Reblatha, (Haydock) so that he could not see Babylon, Ezechiel 12:13.
Sedecias was imprisoned, and buried there by the Jews, Jeremias 34:5, 12.

32:7.text: Next. Priests could sell only to their fellow priests, Leviticus 25:34.

Others might dispose of their landed property to any, until the year of jubilee. The nearest relation had the first offer, but he was not obliged to purchase. Jeremias now consented, to shew that the people should return to possess the land, though it was now in the enemy's hands.
Thus a Roman purchased the land on which Hannibal was encamped, despising his power and threats.

32:8.text: Came into the city clandestinely; or rather he entered before the siege.

32:11.text: Outside. "There was another copy left open for inspection, (ver. 14.) which custom still prevails," says St. Jerome.

32:14.text: Days, safe from dampness, etc. Thus Origen found a version of the Bible preserved at Jericho.
After the captivity, the owners shall claim their land.

32:17.text: Alas. Hebrew ahah, ah! only once. Septuagint, "O, thou Being, Lord God."
"When the gods perform, nothing appears incredible." (Pindar, Pyt. x.) See ver. 27., and Luke 1:37.

32:18.text: After them, to the third and fourth generation, Exodus 34:7. His mercy is more exalted.

32:20.text: Day. We remember thy ancient miracles, and witness others.

32:23.text: Any of. Literally, "all those," which seems better, as he who offends in one, becomes guilty of all; and the Jews certainly had observed some precepts, though they did not persevere unto the end.

32:24.text: By. Literally, "at the presence of the sword," or by its force, Psalm 59:6.

32:25.text: Is given, in the eternal decrees, ver. 28.

32:29.text: Baal, the sun, etc., Jeremias 19:13., Sophonias 1:5., and 4 Kings 23:12.

32:30.text: The Jews had rebelled in the desert.

32:31.text: Built it. The Jebusites polluted it. David himself offended, as well as Solomon, and under all the kings many crimes were committed, (Calmet) which is always the case in large cities. These general terms only denote a great corruption, tough some would be virtuous.

32:34.text: It, under Achaz, Manasses, and perhaps the last kings of Juda, Ezechiel 8:9.

32:35.text: Consecrate. Hebrew, "make them pass" through or into the fire, to be consumed in honour of Moloc, 4 Kings 21:6.
Heart. They perhaps meant to imitate Abraham, Jeremias 7:31.

32:37.text: Lands. What is here said must be understood of the captives, yet in such a manner that Christians are more particularly designated, for the Jews never enjoyed such perfect security, nor followed one way, nor enjoyed the eternal covenant, (ver. 39, 40., and Jeremias 31:31.; Calmet) except those who embraced the true faith of Christ. At all times one religion alone can be pleasing to God, (ver. 39.) and those who were saved under the old law, must have believed in a Redeemer to come, as we must do in one already past.
God would not suffer true believers to become extinct under the old law; much less will he since Christ's coming.

32:41.text: Truth, like a tree which shall not be eradicated.

32:44.text: Juda, and in. A farther description of these cities is given, situated about the capital, Hebron, Sephala, (the plain) near Gaza, (Calmet) and the Negeb or south country, (Haydock) towards Arabia. Here the excellent commentary of St. Jerome ends, to our great loss.

It was perhaps his last work, undertaken after the twelve minor and the other three great prophets. He never wrote on Baruch, on the epistle of Jeremias, the Greek parts of Daniel, etc., as the Jews did not allow them to be canonical, and the point was not then decided. The Church styles St. Jerome "the greatest doctor in expounding the sacred Scriptures," as he translated and illustrated the greatest part of them; (Haydock) and indeed he seems to have been the best qualified for the undertaking, of all the fathers.

Hence we may lament the more that we have not all his works in their genuine purity and perfection, as even the latest editions are very faulty, and proceed upon a plan which the learned do not approve. See A. Butler, Kennicott, etc.

33:1.text: Year of the World 3414, Year before Christ 590.
He was comforted in prison by two visions, shewing that the Church should not perish for the sins of many.

33:2.text: Thus. Hebrew uses the feminine for the neuter. Septuagint, "forming the earth," etc.

33:3.text: Cry. The prophetic spirit was sometimes granted to earnest prayer, Daniel 9:2., and 10:3.

33:4.text: To. "Concerning the houses....and the sword of them," who would not obey God, thus bringing on their own destruction.

33:6.text: Their. The houses, wounds, or breaches.
Peace. That is, the peace and welfare which they pray for.
I will teach them how to pray for a durable peace.

33:8.text: Iniquity, by means of the captivity, which shall be a sort of baptism, Jeremias 31:29.

33:9.text: And it. Jerusalem, as figure of the Church.
Fear, worship, and desist from persecuting God in his saints, (Haydock) whom he so highly favours.

33:11.text: First, when they were settled in it under Josue.

33:13.text: Numbereth them, they come into the fold.
Chaldean, "my people shall be instructed, and formed by the hand of the Messias." Grabe supplies what follows to the end of the chapter.

33:15.text: Bud, or orient, Christ, (Calmet) of whom Zorobabel was a figure. (Theodoret) All cannot be verified of the latter, ver. 16.
The Jews themselves explain this of the Messias.
It evidently refer to him, as he was born of David, whose posterity should continue till Christ, the founder of an eternal kingdom.

33:16.text: Him. (Septuagint, Chaldean, etc.) The Hebrew has "her" Jerusalem, or the Church, which receives all its beauty from Christ.
See Jeremias 23:5., where all read him.

33:17.text: David. This was verified in Christ, who is of the house of David; and whose kingdom in his Church shall have no end.

The tribe of Juda continued most eminent till his coming. But there was no king till Hyrcan, and he was of another tribe. The priests governed after Nehemias, till Herod was appointed by the Romans. This must therefore be explained of Christ's eternal kingdom, Genesis xlix.

33:18.text: Priests. This promise relates to the Christian priesthood; which shall also continue for ever: the functions of which (more especially the great sacrifice of the altar) are here expressed, by the name of holocausts, and other offerings of the law, which were so many figures of the Christian sacrifice.
The Levitical sacrifices have ceased for 17 centuries. But Christ will officiate by his ministers till the end of time.
St. Hypolitus and all the fathers agree that the blessed Eucharist is the complement of all the ancient sacrifices.

33:21.text: Ministers. The promise in not conditional, but as unchangeable as the course of the seasons.

33:24.text: Families of kings and priests, (Challoner) or the two kingdoms of Israel and Juda. The people complained that God had broken his word, (Calmet) so heretics assert that he has abandoned his Church for above 800 years. Providence watched in a particular manner over the families of Aaron and David, which enjoyed peculiar privileges, (Haydock) or blessings.

34:1.text: Year of the World 3414, Year before Christ 590.
Came, in the 11th year of Sedecias, before the Chaldeans returned to their siege, Jeremias 37:4.

34:3.text: Go to, yet shall not see Babylon, (Ezechiel 12:13.) his eyes being put out, Jeremias 32:4. (Calmet) See 4 Kings 25:7.
Paine objects this as a false prophecy, though it was verified so terribly. Daniel, etc., would not neglect to bury the king.

34:5.text: Peace. That is, by a natural death.
Burn thee, like Asa; (2 Paralipomenon 16:14.; Calmet) or aromatical spices were only burnt over their dead bodies, which were also embalmed.
Tostat says that Sedecias was intoxicated, for sport, (Habacuc 2:15.) and died of grief in prison, Jeremias 52:11.
The king of Babylon would probably not refuse him a decent burial.

34:7.text: Lachis, near Hebron. Detachments were sent to different places.

34:9.text: Brethren. The sabbatical year happened in the 9th of Sedecias, when the Chaldeans approaching, caused him to shew some signs of religion. But when they departed to meet the Egyptians, the people repented of what they had done well.
Such a relapse offends God more than the former sins, as our Saviour shews by a parable, Matthew xviii.

34:14.src: Glass.; Gram. 3:6.; Exodus 21:2., Deuteronomy 15:12., and Luke 2:21.; Calmet

34:16.text: And set. Hebrew, "to their own soul," to do as they pleased.

34:17.text: For you. I acknowledge you no longer as my servant. I will let loose the sword, etc., against you.

34:18.text: Parts. This was done to signify that if they transgressed, they consented to be slain, Genesis 15:9, 17. It seems Sedecias and his subjects had solemnly ratified the covenant made at Sinai, being impressed with fear of the enemy.

34:19.text: Eunuchs, chief officers. One was at the head of the army, 4 Kings 25:19.

34:21.text: From you, to attack Egypt. Afterwards the various detachments came and took Jerusalem.

34:22.text: Command. He did not approve of their cruelty, but used them as scourges.

35:1.text: Year of the World 3398, Year before Christ 606.
Joakim, when Nabuchodonosor besieged him again in his 7th year. The Rechabites then pitched their tents in Jerusalem, ver. 11.

Here we behold a figure of religious orders in the Church, as well as in the Nazarites and disciples of the prophets, Numbers vi., and 4 Kings 1:They observed certain rules for acquiring greater perfection, and refrained from many things which were otherwise lawful. Thus various religious orders, both of men and of women, have flourished in the Church, and though they have different names and regulations, yet they are not sects, as heretics would pretend, but all follow the same faith, use the same sacraments, and are a great ornament to the Catholic Church by their profession of the three essential vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience, in imitation of the apostles, Matthew 19:12.
Joakim. This happened before the preceding prophecies.

35:2.text: Rechabites. These were of the race of Jethro, father-in-law to Moses.
All the Hebrew and Latin doctors agree that the Rechabites were strangers, ver. 7, 19.
Treasures of corn, wine, etc. Jeremias, as a priest, had free access.

35:4.text: Of God. Prophets are generally thus designated, 4 Kings 4:7. We know nothing more of this holy man.
Princes of the priests; or where princes made their offerings, or assembled to judge, Jeremias 26:10.
Entry. He was not removeable like the other Levites, 1 Paralipomenon 9:19, 34.

35:5.text: Wine. This was not an exhortation, but a trial.

35:6.text: Jonadab. A man much esteemed by Jehu, 4 Kings 10:15.

35:7.text: Days. The reward of dutiful children, Exodus 20:12. These are models of those Christians who follow the rule of some virtuous person, in order to be at a greater distance from any transgression of the law, and to observe it with more perfection.

"If a father could lay such an injunction on his descendants, and they receive commendation for observing it, why may not the fathers of the Church enjoin things which are not of themselves necessary, but....useful to avoid the occasions of evil, or to advance in virtue?" says Grotius, a Protestant, after St. Jerome, (Ad Paulin.) St. Augustine, in Psalm lxx., etc.) (Haydock) Jonadab exhorted, and prescribed this rule, but it was not properly a command, being too arduous.

35:11.text: Syria. 4 Kings 24:2. The mention of these shews that this was the second siege, when Joakim was slain, and many led into captivity, (chap. 52:28.) with these Rechabites.
They lived under tents, but in case of necessity entered the city, ver. 10. If they observe works of supererogation, all must surely keep God's law.

35:14.text: This was only a counsel, mine a law!

35:19.text: Before me, in an honourable situation (1 Paralipomenon 2:55.) about the temple. It was not requisite that they should be Levites.
For ever. Their reward shall be eternal bliss.
This they sought for, as they were strangers of Madian in the country, ver. 7.

36:1.text: Year of the World 3398, Year before Christ 606.
Fourth, towards the conclusion, after the siege was raised, ver. 6, 9.

36:2.text: Write. He had already prophesied 22 years. Now he was more frequently ordered to write.
He executed the order by the hand of Baruch, ver. 4.

36:3.text: God makes this last effort, that the hearing of so many separate prophecies together might make a deeper impression.

36:4.text: Book. This was afterwards burnt. What he wrote a second time has not come down to us, in the same order. Jeremias made use of his memory, which was directed by the Holy Spirit.

36:5.text: Shut up. Not that the prophet was now in prison; for the contrary appears from ver. 19., but that he kept himself shut up, by reason of the persecutions he had lately met with. See Jeremias 26.
If he had been confined, as Grotius asserts, the king would easily have found him.

He and Baruch retired to some secret place, as most priests (Worthington) did formerly in England, (Haydock) that they might better exercise their functions than they could do in the hands of the persecutors.

36:6.text: Read. This may sometimes have as good an effect as preaching without a book.
Fasting day of expiation, (Usher, the year of the world 3398) or rather on (Haydock) the day prescribed in the 9th month, ver. 9.

36:7.text: They. Literally, "their supplication may fall prostrate before," etc.

It is personified. So Homer represents (Calmet) "supplications," as daughters of Jupiter, lame, and with eyes averted, (Iliad ix.) to shew how we ought to pray. Jeremias finds means to instruct the people: the word of God is not bound, 2 Timothy 2:9.
As many refused to hear his discourses, God ordered him to write what might be a perpetual warning and reproach, or testimony against them.

36:9.text: Fast. Probably on account of the catastrophe the second year, Daniel 1:2. Many such were afterwards appointed, Zacharias 7:3., and 8:19. All came to Jerusalem on such occasions.

36:10.text: Gate, on the east, (2 Paralipomenon 20:5.) leading to the court of the priests.

36:18.text: Ink. It formerly resembled that used by printers, (Calmet) being composed of soot and gum.

36:19.text: Are. Thus they provide for the safety of the prophets, and perform their duty to the king.

36:21.text: Read it. This was the third time. It was in vain afterwards to destroy the book.

36:22.text: Winter-house. He was very magnificent, and had other apartments for summer. Though the climate was warm, fires would be useful in November.
Hearth. There was no chimney used, and the smoke went out by the door, or by holes at the top of the room, as we see in some old monasteries.

36:23.text: Pages. Perhaps what was written on so many skins, (Haydock) or sheets of paper, pasted together, and rolled up; or there might be some marks to shew the different subjects, like the sections used in the synagogue, (Calmet) or our chapters; though we are assured that all was formerly written without any separation even of letters. Protestants have "leaves."
Hebrew, "doors." (Calmet) The secretary cut and burnt the leaves by the king's order, ver. 25.

36:24.text: Afraid. They saw all without any concern, not believing that God spoke to them. They did not imitate Josias, 2 Paralipomenon 34:19.

36:26.text: Hid them, and prevented any one from betraying them.
He did not remove them to a place different from what they had chosen by his direction.

36:30.text: None, etc. Because his son, Joachin or Jechonias, within three months after the death of his father, was carried away to Babylon, so that his reign is not worthy to be taken notice of.
He was subject to the Chaldeans; his uncle took his place. Joakim had the burial of an ass, Jeremias 12:18.

36:30.src: St. Thomas Aquinas, [Summa Theologiae] p. 3. qu. 31. a. 2.; Worthington
36:30.text: None of the following princes enjoyed the glory or power of the ancient king.

37:1.text: Sedecias. He was less impious than his two predecessors; but too weak to do good.

37:3.text: Year of the World 3414, Year before Christ 590.
Pray, or consult. He wished to know whether the Chaldeans would return.
He feared, yet ill-treated the prophet, as Herod did John the Baptist.

37:6.text: Return, routed, Jeremias 46:15. His army was small, Ezechiel 17:17., and 30:21. This king Ephree, (chap. 44:30.) or Apries, had succeeded Psammis, after an interregnum of ten years' continuance.
He foresaw that his own dominions would be invaded, after Jerusalem, the key, was taken. The other allies of Juda perfidiously sat still, or joined the Chaldeans.

37:8.text: Away. They departed indeed, but for a short time.

37:9.text: Tent. So easy it is for God to destroy by few as well as by many!

37:11.text: Yet some think this had not yet taken place.
Septuagint, "to buy provisions among." Hebrew, "to slip away thence in the midst of the people."
He wished to escape the fury of his enemies at Jerusalem.
It is an old device of persecutors to lay false charges on the innocent, as Julian and the Arians did.

37:14.text: Beat him, as a traitor and fugitive.

37:15.text: Prison. Literally, "lake or pit," (Haydock) such as was used to keep wine. Thus God tries his chosen friends, according to their strength!

37:18.text: Prophets. He insults over these impostors, and speaks boldly. The Chaldeans were now returned, Jeremias 38.

37:20.text: Entry, where he had been already, Jeremias 32:1., and 34:1, 7.
Piece. Roll, sufficient for a day's maintenance.
Protestants, "a piece of bread (Septuagint, a loaf) out of the baker's street."

38:1.text: Year of the World 3415, Year before Christ 589.
And Phassur, the violent priest, Jeremias 20:1.
People, who might come to the entry of the prison.

38:2.text: The Hebrew idiom implies that he shall most surely live. Voluntary offerings prevent eternal misery.

38:5.text: Lawful. This is a compliment, or Sedecias complains indirectly that they had only left him the name of king. He grieved at the treatment of the prophet.

38:6.text: Mire, up to the neck; so that he would soon have been smothered.

38:7.text: God moves some to pity the distressed, till he recompense their patience.

38:9.text: City. It was useless, therefore, to add the torment of the dungeon, since he must soon have perished.

38:14.text: Third, or officers of the guard's gate, leading from the palace to the temple, 4 Kings 11:19.
Hebrew hashelishi; (Haydock) the third denotes also an officer of the army.

38:17.text: King. He was at Reblatha. Though an usurper, he had claims upon Sedecias, whom he had appointed ruler, on his swearing to be faithful and to pay tribute. The prophet's advice was just.
Even conditional prophecies are certain, and the king would have been treated differently if he had complied. But on his refusal, great misery ensued.

38:19.text: Jews. Traitors, whom Sedecias had perhaps treated ill.

38:22.text: Say. At parting, bewailing thy blindness, which has entailed misery upon all.
Of peace. That is, thy false friends, promising thee peace and happiness, and by their evil counsels involving thee in misery.
Mire. He alludes to his own treatment, (Calmet) which he had received from these false counsellors or princes, ver. 4.

38:26.text: There. This he had actually done, Jeremias 37:19. He perhaps renewed the petition, at this interview, to satisfy the king.
We may conceal the truth, but must never speak what is false.

"In a matter, says Puffendorf, which I am not obliged to declare to another, if I cannot with safety conceal the whole, I may fairly discover no more than a part." Who can require a privy counsellor to reveal the king's secret? Yet Paine accuses the prophet of duplicity!

39:1.text: Year of the World 3414.; 4 Kings xxv.

39:2.text: Year of the World 3416, Year before Christ 588.

Fifth. The parallel passages and other editions read ninth. Some pretend that (Calmet) the city was taken on the 5th of the 4th month, and after being pillaged was burnt on the 7th (4 Kings 24:8.) or 10th of the 5th month, Jeremias 52:12.
But the city was taken on the 9th of the 4th month, and Nabuzardan entered on the 7th of the 5th month, and burnt it and the temple on the 10th.

From the beginning of the siege (the year of the world 3414) to the end (the year 3416.; Usher), two years and a half elapsed. It was interrupted a short time. Two fasts are still observed by the Jews to deplore these events, Zacharias 7:3., and 8:19.

39:3.text: Neregel, Sereser. Perhaps Nabuzardan should be read, (ver. 13.; Haydock) or these names are mentioned twice, probably by mistake of transcribers. The former was an idol; (4 Kings 17:30.) the latter word means "treasurer."
(Protestants) Nergal-Sharezer seems to be one person, as in Septuagint, or "treasurer of Nergel."
Samgar-nebu, was "prefect of the temple of Nabo."
Sarsachim, "prince of the sacae, or Scythians."
Rabsares, "master of the eunuchs," or officers within the palace. Such a one was employed by Sennacherib, 4 Kings 18:17.
Rebmag, "chief of the magi," (Calmet) or "master of the camp."
These entered the first wall, chap 1:15., and Isaias 22:7.

39:4.text: Walls. This gate had not yet been seized, (Calmet) leading to Jericho, ver. 5.
Roman Septuagint passes over the next 12 verses, which seem to be taken from 4 Kings 25:4.
Grabe supplies ver. 4. to 13., inclusively. "And they sent," etc., which is well connected with ver. 3.
The possession of one gate would let all the army enter: whereupon Sedecias fled by a postern gate.

39:5.text: In the land, or environs of Emesa, in Syria.
Judgment. Reproaching him with perfidy and ingratitude.
He slew the nobles, as his accomplices.

39:9.text: Army. Hebrew, "slayers," denoting soldiers, cooks, and sacrificers, Genesis 37:36.
Remained, having escaped the sword, etc. They had been spared when Joakim and Jechonias were taken.

39:10.text: Cisterns, and fields, 4 Kings 25:12. They were thus attached to the Chaldeans.

39:11.text: Had given, after the prisoners had got to Rama, Jeremias 40:1.

39:13.text: Nabusezban perhaps stand for Semegar, Nabu, and Sarsachim. The other four officers occur [in] ver. 3.

39:14.text: Godolias. The Jews say he had gone over to assist the Chaldeans.

39:18.text: Saved. Hebrew, "a booty," Jeremias 21:9. Thus God rewards what is done to his servants, Matthew 25:40.

40:1.text: Word. This happened to Jeremias; (Raban) or the prophet rather interrupts what he was about to say, to let the reader know the state of affairs, when he was consulted about the journey into Egypt, Jeremias 42:9.
Rama; probably in Benjamin, near Bethel, Judges 19:12.
In this last part, Jeremias foretells what would befall the Jews in Egypt, and other nations.

40:3.text: This pagan acknowledges that God had punished his people, (chap. 18:16.; Calmet) as Titus did at the last siege.
He acknowledges the justice of God.

40:4.text: Before thee. Thou hast liberty to choose, Genesis 13:9., and 20:15., and 24:51.

40:5.text: And. Hebrew, "And as he (the people, or Godolias) will not return, dwell;" or, "He had not yet answered, when the general said: Return to Godolias." He perceived that the prophet was not desirous of going to Babylon; and he insinuated that such a step would not be for his advantage, as the captives would not return.
Presents. He had been plundered like the rest.

40:6.text: Masphath; because it was near Jerusalem, and an ancient place of devotion.

40:7.text: Captains, who had fled from Sedecias, and effected their escape.

40:9.text: With you. They might naturally suspect Godolias, and think that the Chaldeans would seize them as soon as they should know of their escape.

40:10.text: Answer. Hebrew, "stand before," as minister (Haydock) for the Chaldeans. You need not be jealous of my power; and I can answer that they will not hurt you, provided you continue quiet.
He promises to act in behalf of the people, as a governor ought to do.

40:11.text: Countries. They had fled to avoid the taxes, etc., but found no redress, Lamentations 1:3.

40:13.text: Came, having discovered the design of Ismahel, whose ambition prompted him to murder the governor, that he might reign: as he was of the royal family, Jeremias 41:1.

40:14.text: The Ammonites sought the entire ruin of the Jews.

40:16.text: Do not. He did right in forbidding this assassination, which could never be authorized, even to prevent a similar crime. Godolias should have taken precautions: but he was too open, (Calmet) like Eumenes.

41:1.text: Year of the World 3417, Year before Christ 587.
Month; Tisri, the third of which is still observed as a fast by the Jews.
Godolias had given a supper to the traitors, and had drunk plentifully. They set upon him while he and the people were retired to rest.
This happened two months after the burning of Jerusalem, Jeremias 29:2. Nabuchodonosor soon after punished this perfidy of the Ammonites.
Such tragedies are usually performed with the show of friendship.

41:4.text: Second, or next day. All had been slain in the night but some captives.

41:5.text: Rent, as people mourning. Literally, "dirty." Hebrew and Septuagint, "cut."

Lord, at Masphath, where people performed their devotions, as well as they could, Judges 20:1., and 1 Machabees 3:46.) Jeremias had probably ordered an altar to be erected, as Samuel had done, 1 Kings 7:6.
Others think that these people went thither for a priest, to offer what they had brought on the altar of Jerusalem, Baruch 1:10.
It seems many of the people about Samaria were true believers, and even more affected at the destruction of the temple.
Their city had been demolished by Salmanasar (Calmet) long before, and many fresh inhabitants introduced, who adhered to idols.

41:6.text: He. Septuagint, "they." Yet Hebrew intimates that Ismahel feigned tears.

41:7.text: Into. Literally, "slew them about the," etc.
The pit was not full of water, but had been intended by Asa for a reservoir, (ver. 9., and 3 Kings 15:20.; Calmet) when he built Maspha.

41:8.text: Field. It was usual to cover them up carefully.
Avarice sometimes will set bounds to cruelty, when nothing else will.

41:10.text: King's; Sedecias, (Calmet) or some of his predecessors, as all the women were taken, (chap. 38:22, 23.) unless those had escaped before.

41:11.text: Johanan. He and the rest of the fugitives thus shewed that they were clea[][clear?].

41:12.text: Gabaon, famous for the battle of Abner, 2 Kings 2:13.

41:15.text: Eight. He had brought ten, ver. 1.

41:16.text: Eunuchs; officers of Sedecias, who had escaped, Jeremias 40:9.

41:17.text: As. Hebrew, "at the station or inn of Chamaam," the son of Berzellai. (Chaldean) Perhaps our Saviour was born in this (Calmet) "inn," (Eusebius, Dem. 7:4.) which was then deserted.
Egypt. These poor wretches find no repose.

42:2.text: Few; comparatively, though there was a great number, Jeremias 43:5.

42:5.text: Witness. Jeremias knew their fickle temper. They will obey, only if God comply with their desires.
They began with piety, but soon refused to obey the directions given, Jeremias 43:2.

42:6.text: Good or evil. That is, agreeable or disagreeable.
It would have been well for them if they had been sincere, (ver. 10.; Haydock) and determined to comply whether the prophet announced prosperity or the reverse.

42:7.text: Days. The Spirit breathes where he will, and the prophets must wait his good time. Jeremias prayed for ten days in that place. He now resumes his discourse, Jeremias 40:1.

42:10.text: Appeased. Hebrew, "comforted." Septuagint, "at rest." I forget (Calmet) or pardon (Haydock) the past.

42:12.text: And will. Hebrew, "and he will." The king will take pity on you.
Dwell. Septuagint, "return;" which is less correct.

42:18.text: Curse. People can wish no greater misery to their enemies.

42:20.text: Deceived. Septuagint, "done ill in your souls." Your intention was bad. You thought that I should speak conformably to your wishes.
He saw by the prophetic light, or by their deportment, that they were bent on going.
They were doubly guilty in neither observing their promise nor obeying God's decree.

43:2.text: Sinful people easily become worse. They are guilty of calumny, and comply not with their promise, Jeremias 42:5.

43:3.text: Baruch. He had been at Babylon, and was liberated with Jeremias, always speaking with moderation of the king of Babylon, and exhorting the people to submission. This was enough to make the populace suspect. They are incapable of reasoning.

43:6.text: Hence God did not cease to speak by their mouth.

43:8.text: Taphnis, or Daphnae Pelusiae, sixteen miles from Pelusium, (Antonin.) the Hanes of Isaias, (xxx. 4.) and key of Egypt. It was a royal city.

43:9.text: Wall; kiln, or court. (Hebrew) Justice was administered at the gate, and the magnificent throne of Solomon was placed in the court or porch, 3 Kings 10:18.
The prophet preaches by facts, laying great stones in the chief city, Jeremias 19:10.

43:10.text: Servant. The greatest monarchs only execute God's decrees.

43:12.text: Temples. Hebrew also, "the palaces of the princes," as temples occur below.
Captives. The idols share the fate of their votaries, Jeremias 48:7.
Array; or Hebrew, "cover with soldiers the land." Septuagint, "he shall cleanse (Calmet) or destroy."

43:13.text: Sun. Septuagint, "of Heliopolis," where the largest temple of the sun was seen. Egypt was famous for its obelisks, erected by Pheron, successor of Sesostris. Some have been removed to Rome.
Fire. History does not specify the particulars: but we may judge how the conqueror would treat these temples, etc., from what Xerxes and Ochus did afterwards.

44:1.text: Magdal, the third station of the Israelites, (Exodus 14:2.) or another "tower" twelve miles from Pelusium, Jeremias 46:14.
Memphis, the capital of Nome, near the western banks of the Nile, and famous for its pyramids, which are standing, though the town be no more, Jeremias 46:19.
Phatures; perhaps in higher Egypt. Jeremias might go or send to these cities, this his last prediction, (Calmet) to reclaim his countrymen from idolatry.

44:2.text: Inhabitant: or the number is exceedingly small.
It would be well if people would reflect, how those who have given way to heresy and infidelity have been treated.

44:6.text: Forth, drop by drop, Jeremias 42:18. I have repeatedly warned them by punishments.

44:8.text: Gods. Their blindness and malice was inconceivable. They attribute their past miseries to the neglect of worshipping the moon! ver. 18. They may be compared to a mixture of iron and brass, which is put through the crucible in vain, Jeremias 6:29., and Ezechiel 22:18.

44:11.text: Face, to punish grievously, Ezechiel 4:3., and 20:35.
All did not perish, but the far greatest part, ver. 14, 28.

44:14.text: Desire and expectation, Jeremias 22:27. They meant to return as soon as the Chaldeans had left the country: but their hopes were vain. The enemy would fall upon Egypt, after he had taken Tyre and the neighbouring provinces; and those who had not abandoned that country in time, would perish, ver. 12, 26.

44:17.text: The queen of heaven; the moon, which they worshipped under this name, (Challoner) as also under that of the Celestial Venus, (Theodoret) Diana, and Isis. It seems the Jews had engaged by vow (ver. 27.) to restore her worship.
The women more particularly adored the moon: yet all were guilty, Jeremias 7:18., and 4 Kings 23:5.

44:18.text: Famine. They think nothing of the transgression of the law, etc., ver. 23. Could obduracy and blindness go greater lengths?

44:19.text: Worship. Hebrew, "to represent." The cakes had a crescent on them. (Calmet) See Hesychius in selenai.
Husbands. The women are most zealous. But all partook of this idolatry, Jeremias 7:18.

44:21.text: Heart, or mind to punish you for idolatry. Sacrifice could be offered to the Lord only in his temple.

44:26.text: Liveth. They swore by his name as well as by that of idols. God could not abide such company.

Not one of these obstinate Jews shall be spared, ver. 14. Others, like Jeremias, the Septuagint [translators], and the holy family[Jesus Christ, the Blessed Virgin Mary and St. Joseph], no doubt called upon the Lord in the land of Egypt.

44:30.text: Enemies. Herodotus (II. 161, 169.) informs us that Apries reigned fortunately twenty-five years, when he lost many of his men fighting against the Cyreneans, and was dethroned by Amasis, and strangled by his subjects. Usher (the year of the world 3433) suspects that Amasis was assisted and confirmed by Nabuchodonosor, and he might thus fulfill the prophecies, Jeremias 43:11., etc., and Ezechiel xxix., etc. Josephus (Antiquities 10:11.) and Berosus (contra Apion i.) assert, that Nabuchodonosor defeated and slew the king of Egypt, though Herodotus is silent on this subject, his account being communicated by the Egyptian priests, and full of fables.

45:1.text: Book, which Joakim burnt, and sought the life of the prophets, Jeremias 36.
Baruch apprised of the general distress, weeps for it, and his life is spared.

45:5.text: Great: the spirit of prophecy; (Rabbins) or rather to be exempted from suffering, while all the neighbouring nations were perishing.
He seems to have been set at liberty with his master, Jeremias 40:1.

46:1.text: Gentiles, to whom Jeremias was sent, Jeremias 1:5. What follows regards them, (Calmet) if we except the last chapter.
It was thought proper to place these predictions here, though out of their chronological order, to which the Septuagint have more adhered, placing them after Jeremias 25.
The punishment of the chief enemies of the Jews is foretold.

46:2.text: Year of the World 3397, Year before Christ 607.
Nechao. He slew Josias, and took all as far as Charcamis, 4 Kings xxiii. Four years after Nabopolasser associated his son, and sent him to conquer these countries, which he effected, 4 Kings 24:7.

46:3.text: Prepare Egyptians, the enemy is at hand.

46:5.text: Seen. The prophets usually speak as if things were already past.

46:6.text: Away. It will be in vain.
Nechao went to defend Charcamis, but lost "many myriads in the battle," and all the country "as far as Pelusium, except Judea."

46:7.text: Rivers. He alludes to the Nile. The king of Egypt had a numerous army.

46:8.text: City; Babylon, and every fort which shall oppose my progress.

46:9.text: Men. Soldiers formed one of the principal classes among the Egyptians.
Ethiopians. Hebrew, "Cush," in Lower Egypt, on the Red Sea.
Lybians. Hebrew, "Phut, another son of Cham, inhabiting the Nome Phtenethu," Ezechiel 30:5., and Nahum 3:2.
Lydians. Their situation is not known.

46:10.text: Devour. It seems animated, and eager to slay the victim, Ezechiel 39:17.

46:11.text: Of Egypt; or ye Egyptians, who have been wounded. The balm (Haydock) or resina of Galaad was then very famous, Genesis 37:25.
Egypt thought itself invulnerable, and is derided. Its cure was hopeless, ver. 16.

46:13.text: Egypt, under Apries, about thirty-five years after the former expedition, Jeremias 44:30., and Ezechiel xxix., and xxxi. This country was invaded the last, ver. 14.

46:15.text: Men. Literally, "man." Hebrew abir, (Haydock) where Apis has been perhaps formed.
Septuagint, "Why has Apis (Complutensian omits this word) fled from thee? thy calf, thy chosen one has not remained."
He derides the chief idol of Egypt; or he alludes to those who fell at Charcamis, or rather who fled after they had come out to assist Sedecias, Jeremias 37:5., and Ezechiel 30:21.

46:16.text: Dove. Hebrew also, "of the destroyer." Septuagint, "Greeks," or Ionians.
Nebuchodonosor came with expedition, or had a dove on his standards.

46:17.text: Pharao. Septuagint add, "Nechao." But Apries seems rather to be meant.
A. Hebrew shaon hehebir hamohed. Protestants, "is but a noise, he hath passed the time appointed;" (Haydock) or, "the height which the crowd (or God, who raised) hath displaced."
Septuagint retain the original: but the middle word varies in their copies.
All the boasts of Pharao ended in smoke. He lost the proper opportunity, and caused his own kingdom to be laid waste. Syriac, "the disturber, who deranges the times."

46:18.text: He, the destroyer; (ver. 16.) or "it," my word (Haydock) shall surely stand as long as the mountains, (Calmet) yea, longer than heaven and earth.
My decrees shall be put in execution in spite of the efforts of man.

46:19.text: Furnish. Literally, "make thyself vessels of captivity," or pack up what thou mayst want there.

46:20.text: Goad her. Nabuchodonosor shall subdue the country.

46:21.text: Hirelings. Apries had thirty thousand Carians, etc., who were defeated by Amasis.

46:22.text: Brass. Septuagint, "hissing serpent;" lamenting in secret, Isaias 29:4.
Wood. Battle-axes were then used. Great cities and monarchs shall fall, Zacharias 11:2., and Ezechiel 31:3.

46:23.text: Above, or "more than locusts," (Haydock) which destroy all herbs where they light.

46:25.text: Alexandria. In the Hebrew No; which was the ancient name of the city, to which Alexander [the Great] gave afterwards the name of Alexandria; (Challoner) or this city was built near Rachotes, the harbour. "Ammon of No" was rather Diospolis, (Ezechiel 30:14.; Septuagint) in the [Nile] Delta, north of Busiris. Ammon was the chief god adored at No, Nahum 3:8. Alexandrian Septuagint, "I will revenge myself on Ammon, her son, on Egypt, or Pharao, and on them."
Ammon was of their invention, and for this the people were justly punished. It means also, "a multitude."
Kings. Jeremias 42:12. Apries was slain, (chap. 44:33.; Calmet) and his two successors perished miserably by sentence of Cambyses.

46:26.text: Afterwards, forty years being expired (Ezechiel 29:14.) from the time when Apries made his unsuccessful attack on Cyrene, and his subjects revolted.

46:27.text: Off from all countries, (Calmet) particularly from Egypt; (Haydock) on occasion of which country's deliverance, that of Jacob is foretold.
If God would bring the Egyptians back, much more would he deliver the Jews.

46:28.text: Nations of Assyria, Chaldea, etc., Jeremias 30:11.

47:1.text: Gaza, going or returning from Charcamis. Apries also attacked Tyre, and would of course invade the Philistines. They had assisted the Tyrians against Nabuchodonosor, who therefore made incursions into their territory, and into that of Ammon, etc., while the main part of his army besieged Tyre for thirteen years.

47:3.text: Protestants, "stamping of the hoofs of his strong horses, at," etc.
Children. Fear makes one forget the nearest relations, while each one provides for himself, Jeremias 9:4., Micheas 7:5., and Matthew 10:35.

47:4.text: Tyre. See Jeremias 47:4., and Ezechiel xxvi., etc.

Sidon. It had rejoiced at the downfall of its rival, (Isaias 23:4.) and of the Jews, Joel 3:4., and Ezechiel 28:24. Artax Ochus punished it severely; (Calmet) and its own king, Tenner, betrayed his subjects, who were slain by the Persians, or burnt themselves in the conflagration of the city.
Helpers; Philistines, who came originally from the isle of Caphtor, or Crete.
Cappadocia was not an island, or near the sea, (Calmet) but at a distance from the Jews.

47:5.text: Baldness, the sign of mourning, (Calmet) or captivity.
Peace. Hebrew, "shall be destroyed." Septuagint, "cast away." She has afforded no assistance to Gaza.
Literally, "And ye remnants of their valley, how?" etc.
Cut, in despair or grief, Jeremias 41:5. Septuagint, "Remnants of the Enakeim, (giants) how long wilt thou make incisions?"
The race of Enack dwelt in those parts; though ánkim seems to have been mistaken for ankom.

47:6.text: Sword. He is moved with pity to see so much carnage, but reflects that such is the will of God. Nabuchodonosor was his sword or scourge.

48:1.text: Moab. This people broke their covenant with Sedecias, yet were punished for having entered into it, five years after the taking of Jerusalem.
The same war is described by Ezechiel (xxv. 8.) and Sophonias, 2:8. Many words of Isaias (xv., and xvi., and xxiv.) are adopted, though he speaks of what happened under Ezechias.

Nabo, a town at the foot of that mountain, where Moses died, Deuteronomy xxxiv. It might have been famous for some pretended "oracle," Isaias 15:2., and 46:1. This chief city, with the rest, shall be destroyed.
Cariathaim. This city, and most of the others, were repossessed by Moab after the Israelites were led into captivity.

48:2.text: Hesebon, at the foot of Phasga, and one of the strongest cities.
Shalt. Hebrew, "shall Medemena hold her peace."
"Thou shalt be cut down, O madmen."
It signifies "silence." Thou silent city, thou shalt be reduced to a mournful silence or destruction.

48:4.text: Little ones. Hebrew, "to Segor," ver. 34., and Isaias 15:5.

48:6.text: Heath, or tamarick, Jeremias 17:6. Hebrew Haroher.

48:7.text: Bulwarks. Hebrew, "works," or possessions of corn, cattle, etc.
Chamos, the idol of the Moabites.

He was esteemed the king of the country, as the devil is the ape of God. The prophets often deride the imbecility of these idols, Jeremias 43., and xi. The chief idol of Moab shall fall, to shew the vanity of trusting in idols.

48:8.text: Spoiler, or "thief," (praedo) a title which Nabuchodonosor deserved, on account of his unjust conquests.

48:9.text: Flower: an usual ceremony at funerals.
Hebrew, "wings." Protestants, "signs." Septuagint, (Haydock) "that they may know how to return." Let Moab flee.

48:10.text: Deceitfully. In the Greek, negligently. The work of God here spoken of, is the punishment of the Moabites.

Woe to those who spare those whom God orders to be destroyed, as Saul and Achab did, 1 Kings 15:8, 23., and 3 Kings 20:32, 42. The zeal of the Levites, Phinees, etc., is rewarded, Exodus 32:27., and Numbers 20:8.

48:11.text: Lees. The wine has not been disturbed. It was customary to keep it first in pits, (Mark 12:1., and Isaias 5:1.) and afterwards in large earthen vessels. Vina bibes Tauro diffusa: "Thou shalt drink wine bottled off in the consulship of Taurus."
If the wine was weak, the vessels were put under ground.
The better sort was arranged in order, (ver. 12.) in some clean apartment, Canticle of Canticles 2:4.
Changed. He alludes to the wine.
Moab has enjoyed a long peace and prosperity.

48:13.text: Of Bethel. That is, of their golden calves, which they worshipped in Bethel.
Chamos and the golden calves were both taken away, Osee 8:5.
The ten tribes had foolishly trusted in the latter, 3 Kings xii.

48:16.text: Swiftly; about twenty-three years from the fourth of Joakim.

48:17.text: His name, as particular friends, Exodus 33:12, 17. People in the East had a secret name, which they disclosed to very few, to prevent incantations.
Rod. This nation was exceedingly vain, ver. 29.

48:18.text: Thirst. Dibon was well supplied with water, Isaias 15:9.

48:20.text: Arnon, on which river Aroer stood.

48:26.text: Drunk with the wine of God's wrath.
Dash. Hebrew, "roll in," or "they shall clap hands over Moab, in," etc.

48:27.text: Protestants, "was he found among thieves? for since thou speakest of him, thou skippest for joy."
Chaldean agrees with the Vulgate, which is clearer.
Septuagint, "hath he been found stealing from thee, since thou hast fought against him?" Grabe subjoins, "Thou shalt go into banishment.["]
As Moab was related to Israel, his derision was the more criminal, and more grievously punished.

48:28.text: Place. Seek for shelter in the caverns and highest mountains.

48:30.text: Able. He hath attempted too much.

48:31.text: Brick wall. Hebrew Kir-cheres, (Isaias 16:7.; Calmet) "the city of the sun;" (Grotius) Ar, the capital. The prophet joins in the mournful canticle, as was usual, Luke 7:32.
Those who boast of more than they have, are compared to an earthen wall.

48:32.text: Jazer, or "more than for Jazer;" about six leagues to the north of Sebama, which had a rivulet communicating with the sea of Jazer.

48:33.text: Carmel, which here denotes any fruitful vineyard or place.
The, etc. Protestants, "none shall tread with shouting, their shouting shall be no shouting." Hebrew hedad, "the cry" of the people in the vintage.

48:34.text: As a. Literally, "the heifer;" a title of Oronaim, which was proud and ungovernable.
Hebrew also, "from Agelah to Salisa; the," etc.

48:36.text: Pipes. Hebrew chalilim, (Haydock) lyre or flutes. Such were used at funerals of children; (Matthew 9:23.) trumpets sounded for men.
Could; opposing the conqueror of Asia, ver. 30, 31.
Protestants, "the riches that he hath gotten is perished."

48:37.text: Shaven, or "clipt," (Protestants; Haydock) as in mourning.
Such signs of mourning were usual in those countries. The Romans let their hair grow.

48:39.text: Bowed. Septuagint and Chaldean, "turned his back."

48:41.text: Carioth, probably Kir Hares, which the eagle of Babylon seized. Roman Septuagint omits verse 40.
Grabe supplies, Behold, etc., and here, the heart, etc.

48:42.text: Cease for a time, till he regain his liberty under Cyrus, ver. 47.

48:43.text: Fear. It was customary to hand feathers near a wood, to frighten the prey into pits or nets, Isaias 24:17.

48:45.text: There....saith, etc., to the end of the chapter is supplied by Grabe. His copy subjoins, "What Jeremias prophesied against all nations. Thus," etc., Jeremias 25:15.
Hesebon, expecting shelter. But internal broils ruined them.
There, etc., to [verse] 47., is borrowed from Numbers 21:27.[28, 29.?] Sehon had taken Hesebon from Moab.
Part. Moses has "Ar, of Moab, and the inhabitants of the heights of Arnon." [Numbers 21:28.]
Tumult. Hebrew shaon, (Haydock) means also pride, in which sense Balaam uses Seth, Numbers 24:17.

48:47.text: Days. When they, Elam and the other Gentiles, shall be converted to the faith of Christ. We read of no other bringing back, (Worthington) though it might take place.

49:1.text: Melchom, the idol of the Ammonites.

Gad, to whom a part of their country was assigned. After the captivity of this tribe, the Ammonites seized the country, regardless of God's appointment. They joined the Chaldeans afterwards; but the latter could not depend upon them, and sent them into captivity, to revenge the death of Godolias, Jeremias 41:2., Sophonias 2:8., and Ezechiel 25:3.
They had taken the country as their right, as if all Israel had perished; which God resents.

49:2.text: Rabbath; called Amana, Astarte, and Philadelphia, by Stephanus.
Possess; returning first from captivity, and subduing the cities of Ammon, under Hyrcan, 1 Machabees 5:6.

49:3.text: Hai, or Je-abarim. Both these cities pertained also to Moab.
Hedges. Hebrew Gederoth, may be the city Gadara.
Melchom, or Moloc, who claimed dominion over this people. If they had used their reason, they must have seen that he was no god, since the Lord disposed of him as he pleased so long before.

49:7.text: Edom. This nation was involved in the common ruin, for its barbarity towards God's people, Abdias x., Psalm 136:7., and Ezechiel 25:12.
Theman, renowned formerly for wisdom, Job 2:11. Yet at the approach of danger, all were confounded.

49:8.text: Hole. The territory of Edom, from Eleutheropolis to Elath, is full of such.
Dedan lies south of the Dead Sea.

49:10.text: God permits the Chaldeans to plunder all.

49:12.text: Was not so much. There were some faithful Jews, Jeremias 25:28.

49:13.text: Bosra, different from that of Ammon; (chap. 48:24.; Cellar. 3:14.) though this may be doubtful. Could such a city be unknown?

49:14.text: Ambassador; Jeremias: or rather this is a personification of the passions, which would sufficiently prompt the Chaldeans.
A good angel might urge them to execute the divine decrees.
The greatest armies are thus made use of by Providence, without approving of their malicious designs.

49:15.text: Little. Edom was scarcely known, except in sacred history; and it was forced to receive circumcision by the Machabees.

49:16.text: Hill. The mountains were numerous, and full of caverns, ver. 8.

49:17.text: Desolate. The few who survived left their ancient territories, which are now a desert, like most of Stony Arabia.
Hiss, out of contempt. The punishment was so great as to excite surprise.

49:19.text: Swelling. Literally, "pride;" (Haydock) or those banks which the Jordan reaches, when it overflows. They are lined with woods, from which lions rush to devour the sheep.
Over her. What sort of men come to the attack? or, who shall dare to resist them?

49:20.text: Little. Chaldean, "chiefs of the people." Tsáir (Haydock) is supposed to have this sense, Micheas 5:2.
But the weakest of the Chaldeans will be an overmatch for Edom.

49:23.text: Damascus, the capital of Syria, Isaias 7:8.
It was punished at the same time as the rest.
Arphad; Arad, an island near Tyre, or Raphanae.
Syriac, "Rephad....they are troubled from the sea." Nabuchodonosor left Tyre to invade them.
As in. The islands of the Mediterranean tremble for themselves, and all Syria is afraid, hearing of the fall of so many cities and nations.

49:25.text: They. Hebrew, Septuagint, and Chaldean add, "not." Why have they not spared this beautiful city? or, why have not its citizens given it up, to prevent its entire demolition?

49:27.text: Benadad. So the kings were usually styled, 3 Kings 15:8. Septuagint, "the son of Ader."

49:28.text: Cedar and Asor were parts of Arabia; which, with Moab, Ammon, Edom, etc., were all brought under the yoke of Nabuchodonosor.
We know nothing of the situation of Asor, which means "a court."

It might be the flying camp (Theodoret) of the Saracens, (St. Jerome, in Isaias 21:16.) who dwelt under tents like the hordes of Tartars, the Ethiopian emperor, and the mogul. Yet this seems to have been a fixed habitation, ver. 30, 33.
Cedar was the son of Ismael, and Asor the chief city of the Agarens.

49:29.text: About. The enemy shall terrify them, and plunder all their riches.

49:31.text: Alone. We need not fear any resistance. They have no allies.
The ancient Suevi, of Germany, laid waste the surrounding countries, that they might appear more terrible.

49:32.text: This the Arabs did, in imitation of Bacchus.

49:34.text: The Elamites had besieged Jerusalem, under Manasses, (Calmet) and are therefore punished.
They were subject to Aseradon: Nabuchodonosor subdued them after the other nations, ver. 36. Cyrus set his countrymen at liberty, (Calmet) and thus began his conquests.
Pagan historians assert, at random, that they were before subject to the Medes.

49:38.text: Thence. Daniel 8:2. governed at Susa, the capital of Elam, for Baltassar.

49:39.text: Return. They joined the standards of Cyrus, and became masters of the East.
This and similar texts are understood of the conversion of the Gentiles, Acts 2:7.

50:1.text: Year of the World 3409, Year before Christ 595.

Prophet. He had spoken against them in the fourth year of Joachim, and now is more explicit in the fourth of Sedecias, (chap. 51:60.) sending his predictions to be read, and then thrown into the Euphrates. The fall of Babylon was gradual. It was in consequence of her pride and cruelty, ver. 11, 17, 23, 29., and Isaias 47:6.
It had shewn the greatest enmity to the Jews, and was at last overthrown by the Medes and Persians.

50:2.text: Declare. This is grand. Let all the captives publish these tidings.
Bel, etc. Bel and Merodach were worshipped for gods by the men of Babylon.
Merodach might be an ancient king deified.
These greatest of their idols could neither save the people nor themselves.

50:3.text: A nation, etc., viz., the Medes, (Challoner) under Darius, (Daniel 5:31.; Theodoret; Grotius) or rather under Cyrus, who came upon Babylon from the north, after conquering Asia; though he was born to the east of that city, Isaias 61:2, 25. He was a Mede by his mother, and ruler of that nation. He gave liberty to the Jews, as the prophet inculcates ten times.

Desolate. Herodotus, Xenophon, etc., say not that Cyrus demolished any part of the city; but Berosus informs us that he took it without opposition, and levelled the outer walls. Hystaspes did more damage.

50:4.text: Weeping for joy and compunction. Israel returns, as well as Juda.
Thus Joseph wept when he beheld his brethren, Genesis 42:24.

50:5.text: Covenant. They renewed the old one under Nehemias, and never publicly broke it, as they had done. Yet the covenant of Christ is more properly meant.

50:7.text: Not sinned: the Jews were such notorious offenders. But in what had they injured the Chaldeans?

50:8.text: Kids; rams. This comparison was not ignoble. Go boldly out of the city, before it be besieged.

50:9.text: Nations. Cyrus had Armenians, etc., in his army.
Thence, by the bed of the Euphrates, the waters of which were mostly let out into the marshes. Thus the city was taken, while the people were feasting.

Aristotle (Pol. 3:3.) says, three days passed before all the citizens were apprised of its fate, it was so extensive: but this is incredible. If we follow the account of Berosus, Cyrus routed Nabonides, who fled to Borsippe, while he took Babylon and demolished the outer walls.
The Persians lay rather to the south, and to the east of Palestine, (Haydock) if our maps be accurate.

50:11.text: Bulls. You have rioted in Juda, and treated my people cruelly.
In Hebrew four verbs have improperly i for v; but [in] Jeremias 51:34., v supplants i five times.

50:12.text: Dry. The country shall be equally unfruitful. The waters of the Euphrates being let off, gave a passage to the enemy, ver. 9.
It ceased to be the metropolis or mother city.
The whole country was laid waste.

50:15.text: Hand, to form leagues; or she faints, (Septuagint) and submits, Lamentations 5:6.

50:16.text: Babylon was so large, that people sowed corn within the walls.
Dove, or the destroyer; for the Hebrew word signifies either the one or the other.
Literally, "from before the sword of the dove." The power of Babylon is no more.
The king is compared to a dove, for his swiftness; or God is meek, though terrible.
Land. The other nations were set free as well as the Jews.

50:17.text: Bones. He completely ruined the nation, which the Assyrians had left.
They led the ten tribes away, and the Chaldeans took the rest, 4 Kings xviii., and xxv.

50:18.text: Assyria. This monarchy was subdued by Nabopolasser.

50:19.text: Israel; the ten tribes, whose country is specified.

50:20.text: None. Idolatry shall not be re-established.

50:21.text: Rulers: the most potent empire of Babylon.
All. Hebrew, "anathematize them and their posterity."

50:23.text: Hammer. The violent injustice of the Chaldeans is thus entitled.

50:24.text: Aware. Men seemed to rise out of the earth, ver. 9.

50:25.text: Armoury. Fire and war are the Lord's weapons, Job 38:22.

50:26.text: That. Hebrew, "her granaries; trample on her as on heaps of corn, destroy," etc. He alludes to the custom of oxen trampling out the corn, Jeremias 51:33.

50:28.text: Voice. I hear the captives proclaiming this at their return.

50:30.text: Peace, in the grave; (Calmet) or shall submit quietly, 1 Machabees 1:3.

50:31.text: Proud. So the Chaldeans are often styled in the Psalms.

The prophet addresses Nabuchodonosor, or rather Baltassar, (Menochius) under whom the city was taken, (Josephus, etc.) by Darius and Cyrus. He may be the Nabonides of Berosus, the question is so much perplexed. Yet "we are convinced that Darius reigned at Babylon before Cyrus, and took the city after the death of Baltassar."
Most commentators are of a different opinion.

50:33.text: Israel. Samaria had been destroyed forty-four years before the fourth of Joakim, from which period many of Juda had been captives seventy years, till Cyrus became their deliverer, and chastised the Chaldeans.
Both kingdoms had been oppressed by a strong hand, till a stronger, even God himself, delivered them.

50:34.text: Name. He gives victory to Cyrus. Thus the Lord directs all for the sake of his elect, and laughs at the vain projects of men.

50:35.text: Wise men. They were styled Chaldeans, and inhabited a certain part of the city, being employed in astronomical and mathematical observations. They disapproved of those who cast nativities.

50:36.text: Diviners. Hebrew, "impostors." They were nowhere more plentiful, Daniel 1:20. Fortune-tellers were consulted on every occasion. The eastern nations are still much addicted to this superstition.

50:38.text: Drought. Cyrus almost drained the Euphrates, Jeremias 51:42., and Isaias xxi.
Things, fit to terrify children, Baruch 6:14.
Protestants, "they are made upon their idols."

50:39.text: Fig-fauns. Monsters of the desert, or demons in monstrous shapes; such as the ancients called fauns and satyrs: and as they imagined them to live upon wild figs, they called them fauni-ficarii, or fig-fauns.

Maldonat reads sicariis, "ruffians." Sixtus V and St. Jerome, (in Isaias 13:21.) have fatuis, "foolish wild men." Hebrew, "the Tsiim with the iim shall dwell there, and the daughters of the Yahana (Haydock; swans) shall there reside," or "fishermen among the rushes shall dwell," etc.
Ever. Its situation is unknown. There is still a town of the same name, but not in the same place.

50:42.text: Cruel. The Medes will not spare for money, ver. 3., and Isaias 13:7.

50:43.text: King. Baltassar, (though he was succeeded by Darius) or Nabonides, ver. 31.

50:44.text: He will rush into the fold, Jeremias 49:19.
The king of Babylon had ruined many. Others shall destroy him, rushing on like the Jordan.

51:1.text: Thereof. Hebrew leb kamai, "of the heart, rising up against me."
Many take Leb-kamai to be the enigmatical name of the Chaldeans, by a secret combination of letters, (Kimchi; Grotius) as if they were not clearly designated in the sequel.
The prophet expresses more pointedly what he had declared in the preceding chapter.

51:2.text: Fan her. After the corn was trodden out, it was heaved into the wind. This custom would insinuate the distress and captivity of the Chaldeans. Septuagint, "I will send....scoffers, and they shall treat her with scorn, kathubriousin.
They have read (Calmet) zedim for zarim.

51:3.text: Mail. There will be little or no resistance made, Jeremias 50:3.
The Persians denounce destruction to all taken in arms; or, according to Septuagint and Syriac they exhort each other to fight.
Hebrew of the Masorets, "you who bend....spare not."
Protestants, "against him that bendeth let the archer bend his bow," etc.
Hebrew is printed ne tendat tendat tendans. The second word is properly omitted in some manuscripts. Thus (1 Chronicles 24:6.) we read taken taken, achuz having been put erroneously for achad, one.

51:5.text: God still considers the nation as his spouse.
Their land. That of the Chaldeans, (Calmet) or of the Jews.

51:6.text: Silent. Jews proclaim that Babylon is justly punished, (Calmet) lest you partake in her crimes, Apocalypse 18:4. Protestants, "be not cut off in her," etc.

51:7.text: Cup. She has exercised the vengeance of the Lord on Juda, Egypt, etc.

51:8.text: Suddenly. She has not lost many battles; but is fallen at once from being the greatest city of the East.

51:9.text: We. The guardian angels, or Jews reply. Miracles are lost on her.
Heavens. Her crimes call for punishment, Genesis 18:21., and Jonas 1:2.

51:10.text: Justices. We had not injured the Chaldeans, though we had offended God.

51:11.text: Sharpen. He addresses ironically the citizens of Babylon.
Medes. Thus the subjects of the Persian monarchs are commonly styled.

51:12.text: Standard. Call together thy subjects and allies.
This must be explained of Babylon.
Yet all will be in vain, ver. 11.
Ambushes. Herein the valour and genius of heroes was most displayed, Josue 8:2.

51:13.text: Waters. Not far from the Tigris, and divided into two parts by the Euphrates.
Entire, being cut up by the roots, pedalis, (Lyranus) or according to the measure of thy crimes.
Septuagint, "thy end is truly come into thy bowels."

51:14.text: Himself. Septuagint, "his hand" lifted up, or by his power.
Locusts. Their ravages were equally dreaded, Joel 2:4., and Judges 6:5.

51:16.text: Rain. Thunder and lightning are usually followed by showers.

51:17.text: Every man, etc. That is, every maker of idols, however he boasts of his knowledge and skill, does but shew himself a fool in pretending to make a god.
By his, or "by default of knowledge;" (a scientia. Haydock) as the Hebrew may also mean. The Babylonians were so confounded, they knew not what to do.
