Protestants, "Every man is brutish by his knowledge." Marginal note, or "is more brutish than to know," Jeremias 10:14.

51:20.text: Thou, Cyrus, (Grotius) or more commonly the Chaldeans are understood.

51:25.text: Mountain. So Babylon is styled in derision. See Jeremias 21:13., and Isaias 1:10., and 20:6., and 22:1. The city stood on a plain. Some think that its palace and walls are designated.
Burnt; unfruitful. This happened long after Cyrus, though it then ceased to be the capital, and became only a shadow of its former greatness.

51:26.text: Corner. No king or conqueror shall spring thence. Alexander [the Great] thought of making it the seat of his empire, but was prevented by death.

51:27.text: Call together all nations to fight against Babylon.
Ararat, where the ark rested, (Genesis 8:4.) near the Araxes, (St. Jerome, in Isaias xxxvii.) or in the Gordyean mountains, in Armenia, where the Menni dwelt.
Ascenez, or Ascantes, (Calmet) near the Tanais.
Caterpillar, or "locust," (bruchum. Haydock) which resembles more a body of cavalry.
Septuagint, "Push forward the cavalry against her, as a multitude of locusts."

51:28.text: Hebrew Seganim, a title used once by Isaias, and frequently by those who wrote after the Assyrians (Calmet) commenced their invasion.

51:30.text: Those who entered by the channel of the river, would seize the gates to let their companions enter.

51:31.text: King, feasting in his palace, (Herodotus 1:191.) or at Borsippe. (Berosus) He sent to make inquiries, (Calmet) or his subjects hastened to convey the doleful tidings, and thus met each other.

51:32.text: Marches. Hebrew, "sedges," which grew to the size of trees, and were burnt when the waters of the river and lakes were drained. Herodotus (I. 185, 178.) specifies a lake four hundred and twenty stadia square, and says the ditches round the city were full of water.

51:33.text: Threshing, performed by oxen treading, and by rollers, etc., Judges 8:16., and 2 Kings 12:31.

51:34.text: Dragon, or huge fish, which swallows without chewing. Sion is here venting her complaint, Psalm 136:8.
She shews that Babylon is justly punished for her cruelty towards God's people.

51:36.text: Spring; commerce, the source of her riches; or rather the waters shall be brought out of their usual channels. For many ages (Calmet) the Euphrates has been lost in sands, and reaches not the Persian Gulf.

51:37.text: Dragons. This has been the case for above sixteen centuries, Jeremias 50:31., and Isaias 13:21.

51:38.text: Roar. They shall retain their haughty air and threaten others, when they themselves shall fall (Calmet) in the midst of their feasting, Daniel 5:30.

51:41.text: Sesach, the city which worshipped the moon, (chap. 25:26.) Bel, (chap. 50:2.) etc.

51:42.text: Sea: numerous armies of Cyrus, or the waters of the Euphrates let loose.
In the days of Alexander [the Great], many tombs of the kings were inundated.

51:44.text: Down. His priests pretended that he eat, (Daniel 14:11.) and a woman of their choice slept in the most retired part of the temple.
The prophet derides this notion. The idol, or rather his votaries, (Haydock) shall be forced to let go the Israelites.
Fall, by means of Cyrus and of Darius, Jeremias 50:3.

51:46.text: Faint. You may apprehend that your miseries will increase in the midst of such confusion; but no, Baltassar, the last of your oppressor's race, shall be assassinated by Neriglissor, who will be succeeded by Laborosoarchod and Nabonides. This last shall yield to Cyrus, who will grant you liberty. Baltassar reigned two years, Neriglissor four, his ill-tempered infant son nine months, when his followers murdered him, and gave the crown to a Babylonian called Nabonides, who kept it seventeen years, till Cyrus took him prisoner. This we learn from Berosus, quoted by Josephus, contra Apion 1:On the other hand Daniel makes Darius, the Mede, succeed Baltassar, and after him Cyrus reigned. To these changes and continual alarms the prophet alludes.

51:47.text: Slain. Hebrew, "dancers." The people were feasting.

51:50.text: Mind. Offer sacrifices of thanks on Sion, (Haydock) both Jews and other nations.

51:51.text: We. The Jews answer: we are ashamed when we think of these places.

51:53.text: High. Her fortifications and ditches will prove fruitless, Jeremias 48:7, 18.

51:55.text: Great voice, or boasting and songs of joy, usual at public meetings.

51:57.text: Drunk, with the wine of fury, ver. 39., and Jeremias 25:26.

51:58.text: Broad wall. The pagan historians agree not in the dimensions, but allow it was excessively broad and lofty.

Six chariots might go abreast. It was 360 stadia long, (Ctesias); or 480 (Herodotus 1:178.) that is above 23 leagues, allowing 2,500 paces for each. This author says the breadth was fifty cubits of the king, three inches larger than the common one, or about twenty-one inches. Pliny ([Natural History?] 6:26.) improperly applies this to Roman feet, and says the walls were two hundred feet high; while Herodotus assigns so many cubits.
What remained, (Calmet) with the hundred brazen gates, Darius treated in like manner.
Thus was the prediction fulfilled, and the works of so many captive nations brought to nothing. It is asserted that 200,000 (Calmet) daily finished a stadium, (Curt. v.) or 125 paces.

51:59.text: It is no where else asserted that Sedecias went in person, and Septuagint, Chaldean, etc., explain it in this manner. Baruch accompanied his brother Saraias, and probably took the letter, Baruch 1:2. Saraias went to petition for the sacred vessels.

Prophecy, or of the embassy to speak (Calmet) in the king's name. Hebrew menucha, was a caution of Benjamin. It means, "rest;" whence some have inferred that he was chamberlain, (Canticle of Canticles 3:8.) or a favourite. Most translate, "chief of the presents," Septuagint and Chaldean, as if they they had read mincha, which he carried as a tribute to Babylon. Jeremias gave him charge of the parcel, perhaps before Baruch had determined to go.

51:64.text: Sink. The angel did the like; (Apocalypse 18:21.; Calmet) and the Phoceans, leaving their country, swore that they would return no more till a piece of red hot iron, which they threw into the sea, should swim.
Thus, etc., was added by the compiler. Septuagint omit the sentence, as what relates to Babylon is placed [in] Jeremias 28. in their copies.
Yet Grabe puts it in a different character.
Jeremias wrote a great deal, after the 4th year of Sedecias, ver. 59.
He here finished his predictions against Babylon.
This does not mean that he did not write the next chapter, (Menochius) as Cappel allows, (Houbigant) though this may still be doubted.

52:1.text: Year of the World 3414, Year before Christ 590.

Sedecias. This is purely historical, taken from 4 Kings 24:18., etc. Many doubt with reason the Jeremias inserted it, as he could not well be alive at the time when Joakim was honoured, ver. 31. If he had written both this and the Book of Kings, the variations which we here discover would not be seen. It seems, therefore, that Esdras or some other has inserted it, to explain the fall of Jerusalem and the lamentations; as a similar addition has been made to Isaias, Jeremias 36., etc. See Grotius.
The history occurs more at large, Paralipomenon ultra.[last chapter?]

52:3.text: Revolted, breaking his oath, which greatly offended God.

52:12.text: Tenth: 4 Kings seventh, on which day Nabuzardan set out from Reblatha, according to some. But it was above sixty leagues, or hours journey, distant.
He entered the city on the 7th, and put his orders in execution on the 10th.
Nothing of the king's imprisonment, or of the city poor occurs, 4 Kings.

52:15.text: Poor. He took those of the city, and left the poor of the country, ver. 16.
Alexandrian Septuagint omits this verse, which Grabe inserts: 16. "And the rest of the people, the chief cook or general, left for," etc.

52:20.text: Under the sea the bases, (Haydock) or bowls, which, etc.

52:23.text: Hundred; four next the wall were not seen, or were fixed to the chaptrels.

52:24.text: Second in dignity to the high priest, a chief officer of the temple.

52:25.text: Seven: 4 Kings has five. But this seems more correct, as seven were commonly employed, Esther 1:10., and Tobias 12:15.
A scribe. St. Jerome has Sopher, (4 Kings) as if it were a proper name, and not an office.

52:28.text: Seventh year of Nabuchodonosor, and last of Joakim, 4 Kings. Roman Septuagint omits this and the next verse, which are found in Theodoret and the Complutensian edition.
Grabe supplies all from, And Juda, ver 27, to 31.

Zuinglius observes that the Septuagint have not a fragment of it, and that it has been added by some one to hide the ignominy of the Jews. It is interpolated in the Arabic of the Lond. Polyglot, and seems contrary to the true history, 4 Kings 24:14.

52:31.text: Year of the World 3442, Year before Christ 562.
Joachin. He had been thrown into prison when 18 years old, and continued there 37 years. It is not probable that Jeremias wrote this. Evilmerodach had also been the same in prison.
Five: 4 Kings has seven. Probably Nabuchodonosor died on the 25th, and his son then resolved to liberate Joachin; which he did on the 27th.

52:32.text: Kings, who had been conquered, and kept at court for parade.
